meta.purpose: Admission to MBA & business-master's programmes worldwide; in India accepted by ISB, IIM 1-year/executive programmes, Great Lakes, SPJIMR PGPM and a growing list of B-schools

sections.name: Quantitative Reasoning
sections.format: Problem Solving MCQ only (5 options). No Data Sufficiency here (moved to Data Insights) and NO geometry in the Focus Edition.

subtopics.name: Integers, factors & divisibility
subtopics.summary: Properties of integers: factors, multiples, remainders, and even/odd behavior. The GMAT rarely asks a bare 'is this divisible' question; instead it hides divisibility inside remainder problems, 'how many factors' counting, and consecutive-integer arguments, then engineers wrong answers around off-by-one and sign slips.
formulas: Dividend = divisor x quotient + remainder, with 0 <= remainder < divisor
If n = p1^a1 x p2^a2 x ... x pk^ak (prime factorization), the number of positive divisors is (a1+1)(a2+1)...(ak+1)

Divisibility: by 3 if digit sum divisible by 3; by 9 if digit sum divisible by 9; by 4 if last two digits form a multiple of 4; by 8 if last three digits form a multiple of 8; by 6 if divisible by both 2 and 3; by 11 if the alternating digit sum is a multiple of 11
The product of any k consecutive integers is divisible by k! (e.g. any 3 consecutive integers are divisible by 6)
even +/- even = even, odd +/- odd = even, even +/- odd = odd; even x anything = even, odd x odd = odd
keyIdeas: For 'how many factors' questions, always go to the prime factorization first, then apply (exponent+1) to each prime and multiply.
For remainder problems, either list the first few candidates that satisfy each condition and find the overlap, or use dividend = divisor x quotient + remainder to test choices.
Consecutive integers guarantee a specific factor: among any n consecutive integers exactly one is divisible by n, which resolves many 'must be divisible by' questions instantly.

example.q: How many positive divisors does 360 have? (A) 12 (B) 18 (C) 20 (D) 24 (E) 36
example.solution: Prime-factorize: 360 = 2^3 x 3^2 x 5^1. The number of positive divisors is (3+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 4 x 3 x 2 = 24. The answer is (D). Note (A) 12 is the trap you get from (3+1)(2+1) = 12 if you drop the lone prime 5's contribution.

traps: Forgetting to add 1 to each exponent, or dropping a prime that appears only to the first power (here, ignoring the 5 gives the trap answer 12).
Confusing 'number of factors' with 'number of prime factors' or with the sum of the exponents.
In remainder problems, allowing a remainder equal to or greater than the divisor, or forgetting remainder 0 is possible.

subtopics.name: Primes & prime factorization
subtopics.summary: Primes are integers greater than 1 with exactly two positive divisors. The GMAT tests the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic through greatest-prime-factor questions, trailing-zero counts on factorials, and 'is it prime' traps where a composite number (like 51 or 91) looks prime.
formulas: Every integer > 1 has a unique prime factorization (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic)
1 is neither prime nor composite; 2 is the only even prime
To test whether n is prime, check divisibility only by primes up to sqrt(n)
Exponent of a prime p in n! = floor(n/p) + floor(n/p^2) + floor(n/p^3) + ... (Legendre's formula)
Number of trailing zeros of n! = floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + ... (the count of factor-5s, which are scarcer than factor-2s)
keyIdeas: Trailing zeros equal the number of times 10 = 2 x 5 divides the number; since 2s are always more plentiful than 5s in a factorial, just count the 5s.
When counting 5s in n!, remember higher powers of 5 (25, 125, ...) each contribute an EXTRA factor, so add floor(n/25), floor(n/125), etc.
Before calling a two-digit answer 'prime', screen it against 3, 7, 11, 13: classic composites that pose as primes include 51 = 3x17, 57 = 3x19, 91 = 7x13, 119 = 7x17.

example.q: How many consecutive zeros are at the end of the product of all integers from 1 to 25 inclusive (that is, 25!)? (A) 4 (B) 5 (C) 6 (D) 7 (E) 10
example.solution: Trailing zeros come from factors of 10 = 2 x 5, and 5s are the limiting prime. Count the 5s in 25!: floor(25/5) + floor(25/25) = 5 + 1 = 6. The answer is (C). The trap (B) 5 comes from stopping at floor(25/5) and forgetting that 25 = 5 x 5 contributes a second factor of 5.

traps: Stopping at floor(n/5) and missing the extra 5s from 25, 125, ... (here that gives the trap answer 5 instead of 6).
Trying to count 2s instead of 5s, or the min of the two counts unnecessarily; 5s always govern.
Treating a composite like 51, 57, or 91 as prime when picking a 'greatest prime factor'.

subtopics.name: Fractions & decimals
subtopics.summary: Manipulating, comparing, and converting fractions and their decimal forms. The GMAT loves ordering questions (which fraction is largest) and terminating-vs-repeating classification, engineering trap answers around 'bigger numerator/denominator = bigger value' misconceptions.
formulas: Compare positive fractions a/b and c/d by cross-multiplying: a/b > c/d exactly when ad > bc
A fraction in lowest terms terminates as a decimal if and only if its denominator's only prime factors are 2 and/or 5
A purely repeating decimal 0.(d1 d2 ... dk) repeating = (the k-digit block)/(10^k - 1); e.g. 0.272727... = 27/99 = 3/11
Divide by a fraction by multiplying by its reciprocal: (a/b) / (c/d) = (a/b) x (d/c)
To add/subtract, use a common denominator: a/b +/- c/d = (ad +/- bc)/(bd)
keyIdeas: To rank fractions quickly, convert to decimals or benchmark each against 1/2 and 2/3; cross-multiply only the two finalists.
Fractions near 1 are easiest compared by their gaps to 1: 5/6 (gap 1/6) vs 7/8 (gap 1/8) means 7/8 is larger because its gap is smaller.
For repeating decimals, put the repeating block over as many 9s as it has digits, then reduce.

example.q: Which of the following is the greatest? (A) 5/8 (B) 7/11 (C) 2/3 (D) 9/14 (E) 11/17
example.solution: Convert to decimals: 5/8 = 0.625, 7/11 = 0.636, 2/3 = 0.667, 9/14 = 0.643, 11/17 = 0.647. The largest is 2/3 = 0.667, answer (C). Equivalently, benchmark against 2/3: only 2/3 reaches it while the others fall short. The trap (E) 11/17 has the biggest numbers but is only about 0.647.

traps: Assuming the fraction with the largest numerator and denominator (11/17 here) is greatest.
Misclassifying a fraction as terminating when its reduced denominator still contains a prime other than 2 or 5.
Writing a repeating decimal over the wrong number of 9s, or forgetting to reduce (leaving 27/99 instead of 3/11).

subtopics.name: Percentages
subtopics.summary: Percent-of, percent change, and successive percentage changes. The GMAT's favorite trap is treating successive percent changes as additive; it also swaps the base ('percent of' vs 'percent more than') to bait a wrong reference quantity.
formulas: x% of N = (x/100) x N
Percent change = (new value - old value) / (old value) x 100%
A is p% more than B means A = B x (1 + p/100); A is p% less than B means A = B x (1 - p/100)
Successive changes multiply: applying +a% then +/-b% gives overall factor (1 + a/100)(1 +/- b/100)
Net effect of +a% then -b% = a - b - (ab/100) percent
keyIdeas: Convert every percentage to a multiplier and multiply them together; never add successive percent changes.
Pick 100 (or the LCM of the denominators) as the starting amount when no number is given; percentages are scale-free.
Read the base carefully: 'percent of B', 'percent more than B', and 'percent greater than what it was' can each point to a different reference.

example.q: The price of a stock first rose by 25% and then fell by 20% from its increased value. The final price is what percent of the original price? (A) 95% (B) 100% (C) 105% (D) 108% (E) 110%
example.solution: Apply the multipliers in order: final = original x 1.25 x 0.80 = original x 1.00. So the final price is 100% of the original (no net change), answer (B). Check with 100: 100 -> 125 -> 125 x 0.80 = 100. The trap (C) 105% comes from adding the percents (+25 - 20 = +5).

traps: Adding successive percent changes (+25 and -20 to get +5%, the trap 105%) instead of multiplying the factors.
Applying the second percentage to the original amount rather than to the already-changed amount.
Confusing 'X is what percent of Y' with 'X is what percent greater than Y' (the latter subtracts 100%).

subtopics.name: Ratios & proportions
subtopics.summary: Comparing quantities by ratio, splitting totals into parts, and solving proportions (including mixtures where one component changes). The GMAT baits you into answering the wrong part of the ratio or reporting the multiplier k instead of an actual quantity.
formulas: a : b = c : d if and only if ad = bc (cross-multiplication)
If a quantity is split in ratio a : b, the parts are a/(a+b) and b/(a+b) of the total; with a common multiplier the actual amounts are ak and bk
Direct proportion: y = kx (y/x constant). Inverse proportion: y = k/x (xy constant)
To chain ratios a : b and b : c into a : b : c, scale each so the shared term b matches
keyIdeas: Introduce a single multiplier k so the parts become ak, bk, ... then translate each sentence into one equation in k.
When only one component of a mixture changes (water added, some drained), keep the unchanged component's amount fixed and set up the new-ratio proportion.
At the end, re-read the question to output the exact quantity asked (a specific component or the total), not k and not the wrong part.

example.q: In a mixture the ratio of milk to water is 5 : 3. When 4 liters of water is added, the ratio of milk to water becomes 5 : 4. How many liters of milk are in the mixture? (A) 12 (B) 15 (C) 20 (D) 25 (E) 30
example.solution: Let milk = 5k and water = 3k. Adding 4 L of water leaves milk unchanged: 5k / (3k + 4) = 5/4. Cross-multiply: 20k = 5(3k + 4) = 15k + 20, so 5k = 20 and k = 4. Milk = 5k = 20 liters, answer (C). Check: milk 20, water 12; after adding 4, water 16, and 20 : 16 = 5 : 4.

traps: Reporting the multiplier k = 4 (trap-adjacent) or the water amount 12/16 instead of the milk asked for.
Scaling both components when only one actually changes (adding water must not change the milk amount).
Treating ratio parts as the actual counts without introducing the multiplier k.

subtopics.name: Powers, roots & exponents
subtopics.summary: Laws of exponents, roots as fractional powers, and solving exponential equations. The GMAT's core move is rewriting different bases as a common base; wrong answers reward multiplying exponents when you should add, or mishandling negative and fractional exponents.
formulas: a^m x a^n = a^(m+n); a^m / a^n = a^(m-n); (a^m)^n = a^(mn); (ab)^n = a^n x b^n
a^0 = 1 for a not equal to 0; a^(-n) = 1/a^n
a^(1/n) = the nth root of a; a^(m/n) = (nth root of a)^m
sqrt(ab) = sqrt(a) x sqrt(b) and sqrt(a/b) = sqrt(a)/sqrt(b) for a, b >= 0
To solve an exponential equation, rewrite both sides with the same base, then equate the exponents
keyIdeas: Rewrite every term over a common prime base (2, 3, or 5) so the equation reduces to a linear equation in the exponents.
Add exponents when multiplying like bases; multiply exponents only when raising a power to a power. Keep these two operations distinct.
Factor out the smallest power in sums like 2^n + 2^n = 2 x 2^n = 2^(n+1); you cannot just add exponents across a sum.

example.q: If 4^x = 8^(x-1), what is the value of x? (A) 1 (B) 2 (C) 3 (D) 4 (E) 6
example.solution: Write both sides base 2: 4^x = (2^2)^x = 2^(2x) and 8^(x-1) = (2^3)^(x-1) = 2^(3x-3). Equate exponents: 2x = 3x - 3, so x = 3, answer (C). Check: 4^3 = 64 and 8^2 = 64. The trap answers come from failing to convert to a common base before comparing.

traps: Comparing exponents without first converting to a common base (equating x and x-1 directly).
Multiplying exponents when multiplying like bases (or adding when raising a power to a power).
Mishandling sums of powers (writing 2^n + 2^n as 2^(2n) instead of 2^(n+1)).

subtopics.name: Linear equations & systems
subtopics.summary: Solving one linear equation in one variable, or a system of two linear equations in two variables. On the GMAT this almost always arrives disguised as a word problem (mixture, purchase, age, rate) where the hard part is translating the words into two equations, not the algebra.
formulas: Slope-intercept line: y = mx + b, where m is slope and b is the y-intercept
Slope between two points: m = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1)
A 2x2 system a1*x + b1*y = c1, a2*x + b2*y = c2: unique solution when a1/a2 != b1/b2; no solution when a1/a2 = b1/b2 != c1/c2; infinitely many when a1/a2 = b1/b2 = c1/c2
Solve systems by elimination (add/subtract to cancel a variable) or substitution (isolate one variable, plug in)
keyIdeas: Name your variables explicitly and write one equation per fact in the stem; count equations vs. unknowns before solving.
Elimination is usually fastest when coefficients line up; add or subtract the equations to kill one variable in one step.
Read the last line carefully: the question may ask for a single variable, a difference, or a combined expression like 2x + y that you can get without solving fully.
Plug your answer back into BOTH original equations as a 10-second check.

example.q: A store sells notebooks for $3 each and pens for $2 each. A customer buys a total of 12 items and pays exactly $29 (before tax). How many notebooks did the customer buy? (A) 3 (B) 4 (C) 5 (D) 7 (E) 9
example.solution: Let n = notebooks and p = pens. Then n + p = 12 and 3n + 2p = 29. Substitute p = 12 - n: 3n + 2(12 - n) = 29 -> 3n + 24 - 2n = 29 -> n + 24 = 29 -> n = 5. Check: 5 notebooks ($15) + 7 pens ($14) = $29 and 5 + 7 = 12 items. Notebooks = 5, so the answer is (C).

traps: Solving for the wrong quantity: n = 5 but p = 7, and 7 is offered as choice (D) to catch anyone who tracks pens instead of notebooks.
Mis-translating 'total of 12 items' as a dollar equation, or dropping the distribution of the 2 across (12 - n).

subtopics.name: Quadratic equations
subtopics.summary: Equations of the form ax^2 + bx + c = 0. The GMAT mostly wants you to factor (integers chosen so factoring is clean) or to apply the relationships between the roots; it rarely requires the full quadratic formula. Word problems about consecutive integers, areas of numbers, or products lead here.
formulas: Quadratic formula: x = (-b +/- sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / (2a)
Discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac: D > 0 -> two distinct real roots, D = 0 -> one repeated real root, D < 0 -> no real roots
Vieta's relations for ax^2 + bx + c = 0: sum of roots = -b/a, product of roots = c/a
Factored form: if r and s are the roots, ax^2 + bx + c = a(x - r)(x - s)
Zero-product property: if (x - r)(x - s) = 0 then x = r or x = s
keyIdeas: Try factoring first: find two numbers that multiply to a*c and add to b; GMAT numbers are almost always designed to factor over the integers.
Use sum = -b/a and product = c/a when the question asks about the roots (their sum, product, or reciprocals) rather than their individual values.
Always move everything to one side to get '= 0' before factoring; a set to a nonzero constant is a common setup error.
For 'consecutive integer' word problems, model as n(n+1) or n(n+2) and expand to a quadratic.

example.q: The product of two consecutive positive integers is 156. What is the sum of the two integers? (A) 23 (B) 24 (C) 25 (D) 27 (E) 29
example.solution: Let the integers be n and n + 1. Then n(n + 1) = 156, so n^2 + n - 156 = 0. Factor: find two numbers with product -156 and sum +1, namely 13 and -12, giving (n + 13)(n - 12) = 0. Since n is positive, n = 12, so the integers are 12 and 13. Their sum is 12 + 13 = 25. (Check: 12 * 13 = 156.) The answer is (C).

traps: Accepting the negative root: n = -13 satisfies the equation but violates 'positive integers'; discard it.
Answering with a single integer (12 or 13) instead of their sum, or confusing product (156) with sum in the final step.

subtopics.name: Inequalities
subtopics.summary: Solving and manipulating linear (and simple compound) inequalities, and counting or bounding the values that satisfy them. The GMAT's favorite twist is the sign flip when multiplying or dividing by a negative, and strict vs. non-strict endpoints.
formulas: Multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative number REVERSES the inequality sign (a < b implies -a > -b)
Adding a constant to both sides, or adding two same-direction inequalities, preserves the direction
Compound inequality: -a < expression < b is solved by operating on all three parts at once
|x| < a (a > 0) is equivalent to -a < x < a; |x| > a is equivalent to x < -a or x > a
You may NOT divide an inequality by a variable whose sign is unknown
keyIdeas: Isolate the variable in the middle of a compound inequality by applying each operation to all three parts simultaneously.
Watch the endpoints: strict '<' excludes the boundary, '<=' includes it; this changes integer counts.
When multiplying or dividing by a negative, flip the sign immediately and circle it so you don't forget.
To count integer solutions in an open interval, list the integers strictly inside, or use (upper - lower - 1) when both bounds are non-integers close by.

example.q: If -3 < 2x - 1 < 7, how many integer values of x satisfy the inequality? (A) 3 (B) 4 (C) 5 (D) 6 (E) infinitely many
example.solution: Add 1 to all three parts: -3 + 1 < 2x < 7 + 1, i.e. -2 < 2x < 8. Divide all parts by 2 (positive, no flip): -1 < x < 4. The integers strictly between -1 and 4 are 0, 1, 2, and 3, which is 4 values. The answer is (B).

traps: Including the endpoints -1 and 4 because the inequality is strict; counting them gives 6 (choice D), the engineered trap.
Forgetting to divide the bound '8' by 2, or including only one endpoint to land on the trap value 5 (choice C).

subtopics.name: Absolute value
subtopics.summary: Equations and expressions involving |x|, the distance of x from 0. The GMAT tests whether you remember that an absolute-value equation splits into two cases (positive and negative), and it hides one of the two solutions to punish incomplete casework.
formulas: |x| = a (with a >= 0) has two solutions: x = a or x = -a
|x| = sqrt(x^2); |x| >= 0 always
|x| < a (a > 0) is equivalent to -a < x < a; |x| > a is equivalent to x < -a or x > a
Triangle inequality: |a + b| <= |a| + |b|
keyIdeas: Split |expression| = k into two equations: expression = k and expression = -k, then solve each.
For a solvable equation |ax + b| = k, the two roots are symmetric about x = -b/a, so their sum is 2*(-b/a) regardless of k.
Check that k >= 0; if the right side is negative there are no solutions (e.g., |x| = -3 has none).
Interpret |x - c| as distance from c on the number line to reason about ranges quickly.

example.q: If |2x - 3| = 7, what is the sum of all possible values of x? (A) -2 (B) 3 (C) 5 (D) 7 (E) 10
example.solution: Split into two cases. Case 1: 2x - 3 = 7 -> 2x = 10 -> x = 5. Case 2: 2x - 3 = -7 -> 2x = -4 -> x = -2. Both check: |2(5) - 3| = |7| = 7 and |2(-2) - 3| = |-7| = 7. The sum of all values is 5 + (-2) = 3. The answer is (B).

traps: Solving only the positive case and reporting x = 5 (choice C) as 'the' answer, ignoring the negative case.
Adding the two right-hand values 7 + (-7)... or picking 10 (the sum 7 + something) by combining wrong quantities instead of summing the two x-values.

subtopics.name: Functions & sequences
subtopics.summary: Function notation and composition (evaluate f at a value, or f of f), plus arithmetic and geometric sequences. The GMAT uses function notation and made-up symbol operators to test careful substitution, and sequence questions to test the nth-term and sum formulas.
formulas: Function evaluation/composition: f(g(x)) means apply g first, then f
Arithmetic sequence nth term: a_n = a_1 + (n - 1)d, where d is the common difference
Arithmetic sum of first n terms: S_n = (n/2)(a_1 + a_n) = (n/2)(2a_1 + (n - 1)d)
Geometric sequence nth term: a_n = a_1 * r^(n - 1), where r is the common ratio
Geometric sum of first n terms: S_n = a_1 (r^n - 1)/(r - 1) for r != 1; infinite sum = a_1/(1 - r) for |r| < 1
keyIdeas: Work function composition from the inside out and keep parentheses; substitute the whole inner expression before simplifying.
For a sequence, find the common difference d (or ratio r) from two known terms: d = (a_p - a_q)/(p - q).
Remember the (n - 1) in the nth-term formula, not n; the 1st term uses zero steps of d.
For custom-symbol 'function' problems, just follow the definition literally, substituting the given inputs.

example.q: In an arithmetic sequence, the 4th term is 14 and the 9th term is 34. What is the 20th term? (A) 74 (B) 76 (C) 78 (D) 80 (E) 82

traps: Off-by-one: using a_n = a_1 + n*d and computing 2 + 20(4) = 82 (choice E), or 2 + 19... errors from forgetting the (n - 1).
Dividing the term-value gap by the wrong index gap (using 9 - 4 = 5 vs. mistakenly 9 or 4) when finding d.

subtopics.name: Algebraic expressions & factoring
subtopics.summary: Manipulating polynomial expressions using the standard product and factoring identities, without necessarily solving an equation. The GMAT rewards recognizing a hidden identity (difference of squares, perfect-square expansion, or the x + 1/x pattern) to avoid brute-force computation.
formulas: Difference of squares: a^2 - b^2 = (a + b)(a - b)
Sum/difference of cubes: a^3 + b^3 = (a + b)(a^2 - ab + b^2); a^3 - b^3 = (a - b)(a^2 + ab + b^2)
Reciprocal identity: (x + 1/x)^2 = x^2 + 1/x^2 + 2, so x^2 + 1/x^2 = (x + 1/x)^2 - 2
keyIdeas: Before computing, scan for a factoring identity; recognizing a^2 - b^2 or a perfect square turns ugly arithmetic into one step.
Squaring a sum introduces the cross term 2ab; the +2 (or 2ab) is where most errors hide.
For symmetric expressions like x + 1/x, square the given quantity to reach x^2 + 1/x^2 without ever finding x.
Factor out the greatest common factor first, then look for a recognizable pattern in what remains.

example.q: If x + 1/x = 5, what is the value of x^2 + 1/x^2? (A) 21 (B) 23 (C) 25 (D) 27 (E) 10
example.solution: Square both sides: (x + 1/x)^2 = 5^2 = 25. Expanding the left side, (x + 1/x)^2 = x^2 + 2*(x)(1/x) + 1/x^2 = x^2 + 2 + 1/x^2. So x^2 + 2 + 1/x^2 = 25, which gives x^2 + 1/x^2 = 25 - 2 = 23. The answer is (B).

traps: Forgetting the cross term and reporting 25 (choice C), i.e., assuming (x + 1/x)^2 = x^2 + 1/x^2.
Adding 2 instead of subtracting it (getting 27, choice D) after the expansion.

subtopics.name: Speed, distance & time
subtopics.summary: Motion problems relating distance, speed, and time, including round trips, relative speed, and average speed. On GMAT Focus Problem Solving these reward careful reading (which leg is which speed, when they meet) far more than arithmetic speed.
formulas: distance = speed x time; speed = distance / time; time = distance / speed
average speed = total distance / total time (NEVER just the average of the speeds)
for two equal distances at speeds a and b: average speed = 2ab/(a+b) (harmonic mean)
same direction relative speed = a - b; opposite directions (meeting/crossing) relative speed = a + b
unit conversion: km/h to m/s multiply by 5/18; m/s to km/h multiply by 18/5
keyIdeas: Always go back to total distance / total time for average speed; pick a convenient total distance (e.g. an LCM) when only speeds are given.
When distances are equal, average speed is the harmonic mean 2ab/(a+b), which is always less than the arithmetic mean.
For meeting/overtaking, combine the motions into one relative-speed object: closing gap divided by relative speed gives the time.

example.q: A cyclist covers the first half of a 36 km journey at 12 km/h and the second half at 18 km/h. How long does the entire journey take? (A) 2 hours (B) 2.25 hours (C) 2.4 hours (D) 2.5 hours (E) 3 hours
example.solution: Each half is 36/2 = 18 km. First half time = 18/12 = 1.5 h; second half time = 18/18 = 1 h. Total time = 1.5 + 1 = 2.5 hours. The answer is (D). (Sanity check: average speed = 36/2.5 = 14.4 km/h = 2(12)(18)/(12+18), the harmonic mean, not 15.)

traps: Averaging the two speeds to 15 km/h and computing 36/15 = 2.4 h (choice C) — average speed is total distance over total time, not the mean of the speeds.
Mixing up which quantity is halved (splitting time instead of distance) when the problem says 'first half of the journey' (distance).

subtopics.name: Work & rate
subtopics.summary: Rate-of-work problems: individuals or machines completing a job alone versus together, plus pipes filling/draining a tank. A GMAT Focus staple that is fastest when you convert every time into a per-unit rate and add the rates.
formulas: rate = work / time; if a job is done alone in t units, rate = 1/t (job per unit time)
combined rate = sum of individual rates; a drain/leak subtracts its rate
two workers finishing in a and b: time together = ab/(a+b)
work done = rate x time; fraction of job left = 1 - (work done)
keyIdeas: Add rates, never times: two people who each take 6 hours finish in 3 hours, not 12 or 6.
Set the whole job = 1 (or use the LCM of the times as total 'units of work') so every rate is a clean fraction.
For inflow/outflow, treat draining pipes as negative rates and take the net rate.

example.q: Pipe A can fill a tank in 3 hours and pipe B can fill it in 6 hours. A drain C can empty the full tank in 4 hours. If all three are open on an empty tank, how long will it take to fill? (A) 2 hours (B) 3 hours (C) 4 hours (D) 5 hours (E) 6 hours
example.solution: Net rate = 1/3 + 1/6 - 1/4. Using a common denominator of 12: 4/12 + 2/12 - 3/12 = 3/12 = 1/4 of the tank per hour. Time to fill = 1 / (1/4) = 4 hours. The answer is (C).

traps: Forgetting to subtract the drain: 1/3 + 1/6 = 1/2 gives 2 hours (choice A).
Averaging or summing the times (e.g. taking 3 hours as a mid-value, choice B) instead of combining rates.

subtopics.name: Mixtures & alligation
subtopics.summary: Combining two ingredients (or concentrations) to hit a target price or strength, and repeated-replacement problems. GMAT Focus favors the weighted-average / alligation shortcut to find the mixing ratio quickly.
formulas: weighted average: mean = (q1 v1 + q2 v2)/(q1 + q2)
alligation rule: (quantity of cheaper)/(quantity of dearer) = (dearer value - mean)/(mean - cheaper value)
repeated replacement: after n draw-and-replace cycles of x from a container of volume V, original remaining = V(1 - x/V)^n
to change concentration by adding pure solvent, hold the solute amount fixed and solve for the new total
keyIdeas: Alligation gives the ratio directly: the distances of the two ingredient values from the mean are inverse to the amounts used.
Fix whatever does not change (e.g. the amount of pure milk when only water is added) and let the total volume adjust.
Order matters in the alligation ratio: the ingredient farther below the mean is used in larger amount, so double-check which side of the ratio is which.

example.q: In what ratio must tea costing $60 per kg be mixed with tea costing $100 per kg so that the mixture is worth $75 per kg? (A) 3:5 (B) 5:3 (C) 2:1 (D) 1:2 (E) 3:2
example.solution: By alligation, (cheaper):(dearer) = (100 - 75):(75 - 60) = 25:15 = 5:3. So 5 parts of the $60 tea to 3 parts of the $100 tea. The answer is (B). (Check: (5x60 + 3x100)/8 = (300 + 300)/8 = 600/8 = $75.)

traps: Inverting the ratio to 3:5 (choice A) by pairing each ingredient with its own distance from the mean instead of the opposite distance.
Averaging prices to $80 and assuming an equal mix, ignoring that $75 is closer to $60 so more of the cheaper tea is needed.

subtopics.name: Simple & compound interest
subtopics.summary: Interest earned or owed under simple versus compound growth, and the gap between the two. GMAT Focus typically tests short horizons (2-3 periods) and whether you keep compounding on the growing balance rather than the principal.
formulas: simple interest: SI = P x R x T / 100; amount = P(1 + RT/100)
compound interest (annual): A = P(1 + R/100)^n, and CI = A - P
compounded k times per year for t years: A = P(1 + R/(100k))^(kt)
CI - SI over 2 years at rate R = P(R/100)^2
keyIdeas: Compound each period on the new balance; for 2 years just apply the growth factor twice, e.g. (1.1)^2 = 1.21.
For exactly 2 years the compound advantage over simple is a single clean term, P(R/100)^2 — often faster than computing both amounts.
Match the compounding frequency to the rate: a 'per annum compounded semi-annually' rate is halved and the number of periods doubled.

example.q: A sum of $8,000 is invested at 10% per annum compounded annually. What is the compound interest earned after 2 years? (A) $1,600 (B) $1,680 (C) $1,700 (D) $1,760 (E) $1,800
example.solution: A = 8000(1 + 10/100)^2 = 8000 x (1.1)^2 = 8000 x 1.21 = 9,680. Compound interest = 9,680 - 8,000 = $1,680. The answer is (B). (Check: simple interest would be 8000 x 10 x 2/100 = 1,600, and the extra 80 = 8000 x (0.1)^2 is the interest-on-interest.)

traps: Computing simple interest (8000 x 10 x 2 / 100 = 1,600, choice A) and forgetting the second year earns interest on the first year's interest.
Doubling the one-year interest (2 x 800 = 1,600) — the same mistake as simple interest, a deliberately planted choice.

subtopics.name: Profit, loss & discount
subtopics.summary: Buying and selling with markups, markdowns, and margins expressed as percentages of cost or marked price. GMAT Focus leans on the fact that percentages taken on different bases (cost vs. marked price) do not simply add or subtract.
formulas: profit = SP - CP; profit% = (SP - CP)/CP x 100 (always on cost price)
loss% = (CP - SP)/CP x 100
SP = CP(1 + profit%/100); CP = SP/(1 + profit%/100)
discount% = (MP - SP)/MP x 100 (on marked price)
two successive changes of a% and b%: net% = a + b + ab/100 (use negative signs for discounts/losses)
keyIdeas: Assume CP = 100 to turn every percentage into a concrete dollar amount and read the profit% straight off the final SP.
Keep the bases straight: markup and profit are on cost, discount is on the marked price, so a 40% markup and 25% discount do not net to 15%.
Combine successive percentages with a + b + ab/100 rather than adding them, remembering the cross term.

example.q: A merchant marks his goods 40% above cost and then allows a 25% discount on the marked price. What is his profit percent? (A) 5% (B) 10% (C) 15% (D) 20% (E) 25%

traps: Subtracting the percentages to get 40 - 25 = 15% (choice C) — the discount is taken on the higher marked price, not on cost, so the cross term matters.
Applying the 25% discount to cost instead of to the marked price, which understates the selling price and the profit.

subtopics.name: Age & number-relation problems
subtopics.summary: Translate 'ages ago / from now' or digit relationships into linear equations and solve. Less common on GMAT Focus than on Indian exams, but when it appears the whole difficulty is disciplined algebraic setup and answering the exact quantity asked.
formulas: if present age = x, then n years ago = x - n and n years hence = x + n
the difference between two people's ages stays constant over time
two-digit number with tens digit t and units digit u: value = 10t + u; reversed = 10u + t; their difference = 9(t - u)
sum/ratio conditions become simultaneous linear equations to solve
keyIdeas: Define one variable for the present age and write every other age relative to it and to the correct time point (past vs. future).
Apply the time shift to BOTH people in a 'years ago / years hence' statement, not just one.
Answer the exact age at the exact time the question asks — often the trap is a correct number for the wrong moment or the wrong person.

example.q: A father is currently four times as old as his son. In 5 years, he will be three times as old as his son. What is the son's present age? (A) 8 (B) 10 (C) 12 (D) 15 (E) 20
example.solution: Let the son's present age = s, so the father is 4s. In 5 years: 4s + 5 = 3(s + 5). Then 4s + 5 = 3s + 15, so s = 10. The son is 10 now. The answer is (B). (Check: father is 40 now; in 5 years son = 15, father = 45 = 3 x 15.)

traps: Reporting 15 (choice D), the son's age five years from now, instead of his present age that the question asks for.
Shifting only the father by 5 years (writing 4s + 5 = 3s) and forgetting to add 5 to the son as well.

subtopics.name: Averages & weighted averages
subtopics.summary: The arithmetic mean and its weighted form, tested constantly on GMAT Focus QR both directly and buried inside word problems (mixtures, group scores, new-member-changes-the-average). The GMAT rewards spotting that a weighted average lands closer to the group with more weight, not the midpoint.
formulas: Average (mean) = (sum of terms) / (number of terms)
Sum of terms = average x number of terms
Weighted average = (w1*x1 + w2*x2 + ... + wn*xn) / (w1 + w2 + ... + wn)
For two groups: combined average = (n1*A1 + n2*A2) / (n1 + n2)
Average of an evenly spaced set = (first + last) / 2
keyIdeas: Convert averages to SUMS immediately; totals combine cleanly, averages do not.
A weighted average always lies between the two group averages and is pulled toward the group with the larger weight (count) - use this to sanity-check and eliminate options.
For 'a new value changes the mean by d', use: new sum = old sum + (added value); or think in terms of how far the added value is from the current mean.
For evenly spaced lists (consecutive integers, arithmetic sequences), mean = median = (first+last)/2 - no need to add every term.

example.q: A shopkeeper mixes 8 kilograms of coffee costing $9 per kilogram with some kilograms of coffee costing $12 per kilogram. If the resulting blend costs $10 per kilogram, how many kilograms of the $12 coffee were used? (A) 2 (B) 3 (C) 4 (D) 5 (E) 6
example.solution: Let x = kg of the $12 coffee. Weighted average: (8*9 + x*12)/(8 + x) = 10. So 72 + 12x = 10(8 + x) = 80 + 10x. Then 12x - 10x = 80 - 72, i.e. 2x = 8, so x = 4. Answer: (C). Check: (72 + 48)/(8+4) = 120/12 = 10. Correct.

traps: Assuming a 50/50 mix because $10 sits between $9 and $12 - the mean is NOT the midpoint; more weight sits on the cheaper coffee, so answers built on 'equal amounts' (here 8 kg) are the engineered trap.
Averaging two averages directly (e.g. (9+12)/2) instead of weighting by quantity.
Forgetting that the added quantity x also appears in the denominator (the total weight).

subtopics.name: Median, mode & range
subtopics.summary: Positional statistics: median (middle value of the ordered list), mode (most frequent), and range (max - min). GMAT Focus loves 'greatest/least possible value' puzzles where you must satisfy several of these constraints at once while sorting the list.
formulas: Median: order the values. If n is odd, median = the middle term (position (n+1)/2). If n is even, median = average of the two middle terms.
Mode = the value that appears most often (a set can have no mode or several modes).
Range = largest value - smallest value
Median position for n terms: ((n+1)/2)-th value when ordered
keyIdeas: ALWAYS sort the list first; median and range are meaningless until values are in order.
For 'greatest possible value of the largest element', minimize every other element subject to the constraints, then let the last element absorb the remaining sum.
A 'unique mode' of value m means m must appear strictly more often than any other value - this constrains how you fill in repeated slots.
Median is unaffected by extreme outliers; range and mean are - use this to distinguish which stat a question actually controls.

example.q: A list of 5 positive integers has a mean of 9, a median of 8, and a unique mode of 6. What is the greatest possible value of the largest integer in the list? (A) 15 (B) 16 (C) 18 (D) 20 (E) 21
example.solution: Mean 9 over 5 integers means total sum = 45. Ordered as a<=b<=c<=d<=e, the median is the 3rd term, so c = 8. The unique mode is 6, so 6 must appear more often than any other value; the only slots at or below the median available for 6 are a and b, so set a = b = 6 (6 now appears twice). Remaining: d + e = 45 - (6+6+8) = 25, with d >= c = 8. If d = 8, then 8 would appear twice, tying 6 and destroying the unique mode, so d must be at least 9. Minimizing d at 9 gives e = 25 - 9 = 16. List: 6, 6, 8, 9, 16 (mean 9, median 8, unique mode 6). Greatest largest value = 16. Answer: (B).

traps: Letting d = 8 to push e higher (giving e = 17): this creates two 8s, so 6 is no longer the UNIQUE mode - 17 is the engineered trap answer.
Forgetting the median fixes the middle term, and instead treating '8' as the mean or as a value that can move.
Confusing median with mean when the list is not evenly spaced.

subtopics.name: Standard deviation & variance
subtopics.summary: Measures of spread around the mean. On GMAT Focus you rarely compute an ugly SD; instead you compare which set is more spread out, or judge how adding/transforming values changes the SD. Occasionally a clean set is given whose SD is an integer.
formulas: Mean (mu) = (sum of terms) / n
Variance = ( sum of (xi - mu)^2 ) / n   (population variance, the GMAT convention)
Standard deviation (SD) = sqrt(variance)
Adding a constant c to every term: mean shifts by c, SD is UNCHANGED.
Multiplying every term by a constant k: mean and SD are both multiplied by |k| (variance by k^2).
keyIdeas: SD measures distance from the mean: sets whose values cluster tightly have small SD; sets with values far from the mean have large SD - often you can rank sets by inspection without arithmetic.
Shifting all values by the same amount (translation) never changes SD; only spreading or compressing does.
Adding a value exactly equal to the mean lowers the SD (it pulls the average squared distance down); adding an extreme value raises it.
If SD = 0, every element is identical.

example.q: What is the standard deviation of the set {2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9}? (A) 1 (B) 2 (C) 3 (D) 4 (E) 5
example.solution: Mean = (2+4+4+4+5+5+7+9)/8 = 40/8 = 5. Deviations from the mean: -3, -1, -1, -1, 0, 0, 2, 4. Squared deviations: 9, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 4, 16, which sum to 32. Variance = 32/8 = 4. Standard deviation = sqrt(4) = 2. Answer: (B).

traps: Reporting the variance (4) instead of the standard deviation (2) - 4 is placed as a trap option.
Dividing the squared-deviation sum by (n-1) = 7 instead of n = 8; the GMAT uses population SD, so divide by n.
Forgetting to square the deviations, or averaging the absolute deviations instead.

subtopics.name: Permutations & combinations
subtopics.summary: Counting arrangements (order matters = permutations) versus selections (order does not = combinations). GMAT Focus problems hinge on correctly deciding order-vs-no-order and on using the complement or case-splitting for 'at least/at most' conditions.
formulas: Permutations (order matters): nPr = n! / (n - r)!
Combinations (order does not matter): nCr = n! / ( r! (n - r)! )
Fundamental counting principle: if step 1 has a ways and step 2 has b ways, together there are a*b ways.
Complement: (ways satisfying 'at least one') = (total ways) - (ways with none)
nC0 = nCn = 1;  nCr = nC(n-r)
keyIdeas: Ask 'does order matter?' first: a committee/team/handshake = combination; a ranking/seating/password = permutation.
For 'at least one' conditions, subtract the forbidden case from the total (complement) instead of summing many cases.
Break selection-with-conditions into independent choices and multiply (e.g. choose men AND choose women), then add across mutually exclusive cases.
Compute nCr by cancelling factorials early: 9C3 = (9*8*7)/(3*2*1) = 84, not by expanding 9!.

example.q: A committee of 3 people is to be formed from a group of 5 men and 4 women. How many different committees include at least one woman? (A) 64 (B) 70 (C) 74 (D) 80 (E) 84
example.solution: Total committees of 3 from all 9 people = 9C3 = (9*8*7)/(3*2*1) = 84. Committees with NO woman (all 3 men) = 5C3 = (5*4*3)/(3*2*1) = 10. At least one woman = total - none = 84 - 10 = 74. Answer: (C).

traps: Computing 'exactly one woman' (4C1 * 5C2 = 4*10 = 40) instead of 'at least one woman' - misreading the condition.
Using permutations (order matters) and overcounting, since committee membership is unordered.
Leaving the total 84 (forgetting to remove the all-men case) as the answer - 84 is the engineered trap option.

subtopics.name: Probability
subtopics.summary: The likelihood of events, computed as favorable outcomes over total outcomes, extended to combined events (and/or), complements, and dependent draws without replacement. GMAT Focus emphasizes precise reading of 'without replacement', 'at least', and 'or'.
formulas: P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)   (subtract overlap; for mutually exclusive events P(A and B)=0)
P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B)  for independent events;  = P(A) * P(B | A)  for dependent events
Complement: P(not A) = 1 - P(A)   (best route for 'at least one')
Without replacement: the denominator shrinks by 1 on each successive draw.
keyIdeas: For 'at least one', compute 1 - P(none); direct enumeration is usually longer and error-prone.
Distinguish with vs without replacement: without replacement, both the numerator count and the total drop after each draw.
'Same color / both / all' problems: sum the mutually exclusive cases (both red + both blue), each computed as a product of sequential probabilities.
Multiply along a single sequence of events (AND); add across separate mutually exclusive sequences (OR).

example.q: A bag contains 4 red marbles and 6 blue marbles. If two marbles are drawn at random without replacement, what is the probability that both marbles are the same color? (A) 1/3 (B) 2/5 (C) 7/15 (D) 8/15 (E) 3/5
example.solution: There are two ways to get the same color. P(both red) = (4/10)(3/9) = 12/90. P(both blue) = (6/10)(5/9) = 30/90. These cases are mutually exclusive, so add: 12/90 + 30/90 = 42/90 = 7/15. Answer: (C).

traps: Treating the draws as WITH replacement (using 4/10*4/10 + 6/10*6/10 = 52/100 = 13/25) - the second denominator must be 9, not 10.
Computing only one color (e.g. both blue = 1/3) and stopping - 1/3 is placed as a trap option.
Adding probabilities of overlapping events without subtracting the overlap in P(A or B) problems.

subtopics.name: Sets & Venn diagrams
subtopics.summary: Overlapping-group counting via the inclusion-exclusion principle, usually with a 'neither' category. GMAT Focus tests two-set and occasionally three-set problems, and distinguishes 'only A', 'both', and 'at least one' precisely.
formulas: Two sets: |A or B| = |A| + |B| - |A and B|
Total = |A| + |B| - |A and B| + (neither)
Three sets: |A or B or C| = |A|+|B|+|C| - |A and B| - |A and B... pairwise sums| + |A and B and C|  (subtract each pairwise overlap, add back the triple overlap)
Only A = |A| - (elements A shares with other sets);  for two sets, only A = |A| - |A and B|
keyIdeas: Set up Total = (at least one) + (neither); solve for whichever piece is unknown.
Sort the given numbers into 'only', 'both', and 'neither' regions of a Venn diagram before answering - the question usually targets one specific region.
Inclusion-exclusion exists to correct double-counting: elements in the overlap were counted in both |A| and |B|, so subtract the overlap once.
For three sets, remember the triple overlap is subtracted three times by the pairwise terms, so it must be added back once.

example.q: Of 120 students, 70 study Spanish, 45 study French, and 15 study neither language. How many students study both Spanish and French? (A) 5 (B) 10 (C) 15 (D) 20 (E) 25
example.solution: Students studying at least one language = 120 - 15 (neither) = 105. By inclusion-exclusion, |Spanish or French| = |Spanish| + |French| - |both|, so 105 = 70 + 45 - |both| = 115 - |both|. Thus |both| = 115 - 105 = 10. Answer: (B).

traps: Ignoring the 15 'neither' students and using 120 as 'at least one' (giving 70+45-120 = -5, an impossible value that signals the mistake).
Confusing 'both' with 'only one language' - the question asks for the overlap, not only-Spanish (60) or only-French (35).
In three-set problems, forgetting to add back the triple-overlap after subtracting the pairwise overlaps.

sections.name: Verbal Reasoning
sections.format: Reading Comprehension + Critical Reasoning only (5-option MCQ). Sentence Correction was REMOVED in the Focus Edition.

subtopics.name: Main idea & primary purpose
subtopics.summary: Asks for the passage's central point or the author's overriding reason for writing (to describe, argue, critique, resolve, etc.). Nearly every GMAT Focus RC passage carries at least one of these, and the credited answer must cover the WHOLE passage, not one paragraph.
keyIdeas: Answer must span the entire passage; if a choice only fits one paragraph or one detail, it is a trap.
Match the VERB: 'describe/explain' vs 'argue/advocate' vs 'refute/critique'. A descriptive passage rarely has an 'advocate' answer, and vice versa.
Predict the main idea in your own words BEFORE reading choices, then eliminate anything too narrow, too broad, or the wrong scope.
Watch for the older-view/newer-view structure: the main point is usually the shift or the author's stance on it, not either view alone.

example.q:
Historians long attributed the rapid growth of nineteenth-century American cities primarily to industrial employment. Recent scholarship, however, suggests that improvements in municipal water and sewer systems, by reducing mortality, contributed at least as significantly to urban population gains. Some researchers now argue that public-health infrastructure, rather than factory jobs alone, deserves primary credit.

The primary purpose of the passage is to
(A) argue that industrial employment played no role in nineteenth-century urban growth
(B) describe a shift in scholarly explanation for a historical phenomenon
(C) compare mortality rates in cities with and without modern sewer systems
(D) advocate for increased public investment in municipal water infrastructure
(E) reconcile two competing theories into a single unified account
example.solution: The passage lays out an older explanation (industrial employment) and then reports that recent scholarship credits public-health infrastructure 'at least as significantly.' Its work is to describe that change in explanation, so (B) is credited. (A) is too extreme: the passage says 'factory jobs alone' and 'at least as significantly,' never that industry played NO role. (C) names a detail the passage does not actually supply (no rates are compared) and is far too narrow to be the purpose. (D) turns a historical account into a present-day policy recommendation the author never makes. (E) fails because the author reports a challenge to the old view, not a reconciliation of the two into one account.

traps: Extreme answers ('no role,' 'entirely,' 'proves') that overstate a measured passage.
Too-narrow choices that correctly describe one sentence or paragraph but not the whole passage.
Wrong-verb choices: labeling a neutral, descriptive passage as one that 'advocates' or 'refutes.'

subtopics.name: Inference
subtopics.summary: Asks what MUST be true given the passage, or what the author would most likely agree with, without being stated outright. The credited answer follows necessarily from the text; on the GMAT it is almost always a small, safe logical step, not a bold prediction.
keyIdeas: Credited inference = 'must be true,' not 'could be true' or 'is probably true.' If you can imagine the passage true and the choice false, eliminate it.
Stay inside the passage's scope: reject choices that add outside knowledge, new comparisons, or causes/quantities the text never gives.
Contrast words ('until,' 'however,' 'unlike') are inference gold: what one side asserts implies its opposite about the other side.
Prefer weak, hedged language ('some,' 'at least,' 'need not') over strong absolutes when picking among choices.

example.q:
Until the 1970s, marine biologists assumed that deep-sea ecosystems depended entirely on organic matter drifting down from sunlit surface waters. The discovery of hydrothermal vent communities, which thrive on chemical energy from the seafloor, forced a reconsideration of this assumption.

The passage implies which of the following about deep-sea ecosystems as they were understood before the 1970s?
(A) They were believed to be incapable of supporting complex life.
(B) They were thought to rely on energy that originated near the ocean surface.
(C) They were considered less biologically diverse than surface ecosystems.
(D) They were assumed to derive their energy primarily from the seafloor.
(E) They were known to contain organisms that use chemical energy.
example.solution: Before the 1970s, scientists assumed deep-sea ecosystems depended 'entirely on organic matter drifting down from sunlit surface waters.' If the food supply drifts down from sunlit surface waters, then its energy originated near the surface, so (B) must be true. (A) adds an unsupported claim about 'complex life' and incapability. (C) introduces a diversity comparison the passage never makes. (D) states the NEW, post-vent understanding (energy from the seafloor) and so contradicts the pre-1970s assumption the question asks about. (E) is contradicted outright: chemical-energy communities were DISCOVERED in the 1970s, so they were not 'known' before.

traps: 'Could be true'/plausible-sounding choices that are not forced by the text.
Reversal traps that state the new finding when the question asks about the old belief (or vice versa).
Choices that smuggle in outside knowledge or a comparison/quantity the passage never provides.

subtopics.name: Supporting detail & fact
subtopics.summary: Asks what the passage explicitly states ('According to the passage...'). Unlike inference, the answer is directly stated, but it is paraphrased, and traps are built from nearby true-sounding statements the passage never actually makes.
keyIdeas: Go back and locate the exact sentence; do not answer from memory. The right choice is a paraphrase of specific text.
Distractors reuse the passage's own words in a false relationship (right words, wrong claim) — verify the whole statement, not just the keywords.
Watch direction/comparison words: 'more/less,' 'before/after,' 'causes/results from' are the most common places a distractor flips the fact.
'According to the passage' means stated, not implied — do not reason beyond the text.

example.q:
The monarch butterfly's annual migration spans up to 3,000 miles, yet no single butterfly completes the round trip. Instead, the journey unfolds over three to four generations: the butterflies that begin the northward spring migration are the great-grandparents of those that return south in autumn. Only the final, southbound generation lives up to eight months, long enough to overwinter in Mexico; earlier generations live only a few weeks.

According to the passage, which of the following is true of the generation of monarchs that migrates south in autumn?
(A) It completes the entire round-trip migration of up to 3,000 miles.
(B) It has a longer lifespan than the generations of that year that preceded it.
(C) It consists of the great-grandparents of the spring migrants.
(D) It begins the northward migration in spring.
(E) It typically lives no more than a few weeks.
example.solution: The passage states the final, southbound generation lives up to eight months while 'earlier generations live only a few weeks,' so the autumn generation lives longer than the earlier ones — (B) is directly supported. (A) contradicts 'no single butterfly completes the round trip.' (C) reverses the family relationship: the SPRING migrants are the great-grandparents OF the returning autumn butterflies, not the other way around. (D) is wrong because the northward spring migration is begun by a different (earlier) generation. (E) states the lifespan of the EARLIER generations, not the southbound one, so it misassigns the detail.

traps: Reversed relationships (who is whose ancestor, what causes what, which is larger).
Detail-swap: attributing to one group a fact the passage stated about another group.
Extreme quantifiers ('entirely,' 'always,' 'never') attached to a hedged statement in the text.

subtopics.name: Function & structure
subtopics.summary: Asks WHY the author included a particular sentence, example, or paragraph, or how the passage is organized ('serves primarily to,' 'in order to'). The answer is the rhetorical role — to support, rebut, illustrate, concede, qualify — not the content itself.
keyIdeas: Answer the question 'why is this here?' — the role, not a restatement of what the detail says.
Read the sentences immediately before and after the cited part; function is defined by how it connects to the surrounding argument.
Learn the role verbs: support, illustrate, rebut/counter, concede, qualify, introduce, contrast. Match the choice's verb to the logic.
An example given by one side usually functions to advance THAT side's claim or to answer the other side — track who is speaking.

example.q:
Proponents of the new zoning ordinance contend that it will lower housing costs by permitting denser construction. Critics counter that increased density, absent new transit investment, will merely intensify congestion. The ordinance's supporters, however, point to three comparable cities where density rose while commute times fell — evidence, they say, that congestion need not follow density.

In the passage, the reference to 'three comparable cities' functions primarily to
(A) concede a genuine limitation of the proposed zoning ordinance
(B) rebut the critics' objection by offering counterevidence
(C) introduce a new argument against adopting the ordinance
(D) explain why additional transit investment is necessary
(E) summarize the central disagreement between the two sides
example.solution: The critics claim density will intensify congestion; the supporters then cite three cities where density rose but commute times fell, offered as 'evidence' against that claim. Its role is therefore to rebut the critics with counterevidence — (B). (A) is the opposite of its function: the example defends, not concedes. (C) misidentifies the side — the example argues FOR the ordinance, not against it. (D) inverts the point: the cities are meant to show congestion 'need not follow,' undercutting the claim that transit investment is required, not explaining its necessity. (E) mislabels a specific piece of evidence as a neutral summary of the debate.

traps: Choices that restate what the detail SAYS instead of why it is there.
Wrong-side traps: assigning the detail to the opposing party's argument.
Swapping concede/support or support/rebut — right topic, wrong rhetorical direction.

subtopics.name: Tone & author's attitude
subtopics.summary: Asks how the author feels about the subject (approving, skeptical, neutral, critical). GMAT Focus authors are almost always measured, so the credited tone is usually mild and qualified; strongly emotional or one-sided labels are typically traps.
keyIdeas: Anchor tone to evaluative wording — praise/criticism cues, hedges ('certainly, but'), and concessions signal a balanced, qualified attitude.
Prefer moderate two-part answers ('measured skepticism,' 'qualified approval') over pure-emotion extremes ('contempt,' 'enthusiasm,' 'outrage').
A passage that both grants a merit AND flags a flaw indicates a mixed/qualified stance, not wholesale approval or dismissal.
If the passage is purely reportorial with no evaluative words, the answer is neutral/objective — do not read in emotion that is not there.

example.q:
The latest biography hails its subject as a self-made visionary. Yet the author glosses over the substantial inherited wealth that financed the entrepreneur's early ventures, and treats every setback as the work of jealous rivals rather than of the subject's own miscalculations. The result is engaging, certainly — but readers seeking a balanced account will have to look elsewhere.

The author's attitude toward the biography can best be described as
(A) unqualified admiration
(B) dismissive contempt
(C) measured skepticism
(D) indifferent detachment
(E) bitter resentment
example.solution: The author grants a merit ('engaging, certainly') but faults the book for glossing over inherited wealth and shifting blame for setbacks, concluding it is not balanced. That is a mixed, critical-but-controlled stance: measured skepticism, (C). (A) ignores the sustained criticism. (B) overshoots — the author concedes the book is engaging, so this is not contempt or outright dismissal. (D) is wrong because the author is clearly engaged and evaluative, not indifferent. (E) attributes an intense personal emotion ('bitter,' 'resentment') that the controlled, analytical wording does not support.

traps: Pure-emotion extremes ('contempt,' 'resentment,' 'enthusiasm') where the text is measured.
One-sided labels ('unqualified admiration') that ignore a concession or a criticism.
Reading emotion into a neutral, purely descriptive passage.

subtopics.name: Application & extension
subtopics.summary: Gives a principle, criterion, or pattern from the passage and asks which NEW situation best fits it (or, occasionally, which new case the argument does not cover). Success depends on abstracting the passage's rule and testing each option against it.
keyIdeas: Distill the passage's principle into a one-line test, then check each choice against that test literally.
The credited answer satisfies EVERY condition of the principle; a near-miss that meets most but not all conditions is the classic trap.
Right answers often come from a totally different subject area than the passage — do not reject a choice just because its topic feels unrelated.
For 'which would the author consider an exception/does NOT apply,' invert the test and find the case that violates one condition.

example.q:
A firm exhibits a 'network effect' when each additional user makes the product more valuable to existing users, as with a telephone system: one phone is useless if no one else owns one, but the network grows more useful as it expands. Products with strong network effects tend to concentrate market share, because users gravitate toward whichever platform already has the most participants.

Based on the passage, which of the following products would most likely exhibit a strong network effect?
(A) A brand of bottled water sold in single-serving bottles
(B) A messaging platform whose usefulness depends on how many of a user's contacts can be reached through it
(C) A limited-edition art print sold to a single private collector
(D) A prescription medication taken privately by individual patients
(E) A hardcover novel read independently by each of its readers
example.solution: The principle: a product has a network effect when its value to each user RISES as more people use it. Test each choice. (B) fits exactly — a messaging platform is more useful the more of your contacts are reachable on it, so more users increase every user's value. That is credited. (A) bottled water's value to a buyer is independent of how many others buy it. (C) a single collector means there are no other users whose participation could add value. (D) medication is taken privately; one patient's use does not raise its value to another. (E) a novel is read independently, so additional readers do not make it more valuable to existing readers. Only (B) satisfies the 'more users, more value' condition.

traps: Near-miss choices that share the passage's topic but fail one condition of the principle.
Rejecting the correct answer because its subject matter differs from the passage's example.
Choosing a merely 'popular' or 'widely used' product when the principle actually requires value to increase WITH the number of users.

subtopics.name: Assumption
subtopics.summary: You must find the unstated premise the argument NEEDS to be true to hold together. On the GMAT Focus Verbal section this is one of the most common CR types; the correct answer is a small, necessary bridge between the evidence and the conclusion, never a big new claim.
keyIdeas: Split the argument into conclusion and premises, then ask 'what gap must be filled for the premises to force the conclusion?'
Use the Negation Test: negate the answer choice. If the negated version destroys the argument, it is the required assumption; if the argument survives, it is not necessary.
The right answer is usually modest ('will not drop enough to...', 'no other factor...'), not sweeping. Distrust extreme words like 'all', 'only', 'most' unless the logic truly demands them.
Watch for the two classic gaps: per-unit vs. total (margin vs. volume) and the assumption that no alternative cause is at work.

example.q:
The Bayview Cafe plans to increase its profits by closing its dining room and switching entirely to takeout. The cafe's managers note that each takeout order currently earns a higher profit margin than each dine-in order, so serving only takeout will raise the cafe's overall profits.

The argument depends on which of the following assumptions?
(A) Takeout food is less expensive for the cafe to prepare than dine-in food.
(B) Eliminating the dining room will not reduce the cafe's total sales by enough to outweigh the higher per-order profit margin on takeout.
(C) No competing restaurant nearby offers a takeout service comparable to the cafe's.
(D) The cafe's staff can prepare takeout orders more quickly than dine-in orders.
(E) Most of the cafe's current customers already prefer takeout to dining in.
example.solution: Conclusion: switching to takeout-only will raise overall profits. Evidence: each takeout order has a higher profit margin than each dine-in order. The gap is per-order margin vs. TOTAL profit: higher margin per order does not guarantee higher total profit if closing the dining room drives many customers away and total volume collapses. (B) fills exactly that gap. Negate it: 'eliminating the dining room WILL cut total sales by enough to outweigh the higher margin' — then profits fall and the conclusion dies, so (B) is required. (A) merely restates information already implied by the margin premise; it adds no necessary bridge. (C) is too strong and out of scope — the plan can still work even if competitors offer takeout. (D) speed is irrelevant to the profit conclusion. (E) is not needed: even if most customers prefer dine-in, the plan can succeed as long as enough of them still order takeout, which is what (B) captures more precisely.
options: (A) Takeout food is less expensive for the cafe to prepare than dine-in food.
(B) Eliminating the dining room will not reduce the cafe's total sales by enough to outweigh the higher per-order profit margin on takeout.
(C) No competing restaurant nearby offers a takeout service comparable to the cafe's.
(D) The cafe's staff can prepare takeout orders more quickly than dine-in orders.
(E) Most of the cafe's current customers already prefer takeout to dining in.

traps: Restatement trap: a choice that just repeats a premise in new words feels safe but is not an assumption (it adds no new required link).
Too-strong scope: 'no competitor', 'all customers', 'only' — the argument rarely needs an absolute; the negation test exposes these as unnecessary.
Right-topic-wrong-direction: a choice that would actually weaken the plan (or is merely a bonus, not a requirement).

subtopics.name: Strengthen
subtopics.summary: You add a new fact that makes the conclusion MORE likely to be true — typically by confirming the argument's assumption or, in causal arguments, by ruling out an alternative explanation. The GMAT loves cause-and-effect conclusions drawn from a correlation; the best strengthener usually eliminates a competing cause.
keyIdeas: Identify the conclusion and the assumption; the credited answer defends that assumption.
For 'X caused Y' arguments, strengthen by ruling out other causes, showing the cause preceded the effect, or presenting a control group where the cause was absent and the effect did not occur.
The answer need not prove the conclusion — it only has to push the needle. Compare choices by which moves it more.
Ignore choices that are merely consistent with the conclusion but add no support (background facts, restatements).

example.q:
Over the past year, GreenLeaf Grocery posted small signs describing the environmental benefits of plant-based foods next to its produce section. Sales of plant-based foods at the store rose 30% during that year. GreenLeaf concludes that the signs caused the increase and plans to add similar signs in all of its stores.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?
(A) GreenLeaf's total grocery sales rose about 5% over the same year.
(B) At other grocery stores in the same region that did not post such signs, sales of plant-based foods stayed roughly flat over the same year.
(C) Many GreenLeaf customers say they enjoy the taste of plant-based foods.
(D) The signs were printed on recycled paper to reflect their message.
(E) GreenLeaf's plant-based foods are priced higher than the comparable animal products it sells.
example.solution: The conclusion is causal: the signs caused the 30% jump. The main threat is an alternative cause — maybe a region-wide surge in demand for plant-based foods lifted sales everywhere. (B) is a control group: comparable stores WITHOUT the signs saw flat sales, so the region-wide-trend explanation is undercut and the signs stand out as the likely cause. That directly strengthens. (A) shows plant-based sales outpaced overall sales, which is mildly suggestive but does not rule out that a general plant-based food trend (not the signs) drove the gain — weaker than (B). (C) is background that does not tie the rise to the signs. (D) is irrelevant to what caused the sales change. (E) if anything works against the claim, since higher prices would tend to suppress sales. Best answer: (B).
options: (A) GreenLeaf's total grocery sales rose about 5% over the same year.
(B) At other grocery stores in the same region that did not post such signs, sales of plant-based foods stayed roughly flat over the same year.
(C) Many GreenLeaf customers say they enjoy the taste of plant-based foods.
(D) The signs were printed on recycled paper to reflect their message.
(E) GreenLeaf's plant-based foods are priced higher than the comparable animal products it sells.

traps: Half-strengtheners: a choice consistent with the conclusion but that leaves the main alternative cause standing (like (A)) will lose to one that eliminates it.
Background restatement: pleasant, on-topic facts (taste, packaging) that add no logical support.
Reverse-direction: a fact that actually undercuts the conclusion slipped in among the options.

subtopics.name: Weaken
subtopics.summary: You add a new fact that makes the conclusion LESS likely — most powerfully by exposing an alternative cause, a confounding variable, or a broken link between evidence and conclusion. The GMAT's favorite weakener attacks a causal claim built on a correlation by pointing to a lurking third factor.
keyIdeas: Attack the conclusion, not a premise you must accept as given. Ask 'what would have to be true for this conclusion to fail?'
For causal claims, the strongest weakener names a plausible alternative cause or shows the two groups differed in another relevant way (selection bias / confounder).
Do not fall for choices that merely fail to strengthen; a real weakener actively cuts against the conclusion.
Beware misreading the exact claim — 'reduces risk' means fewer, not zero; a choice noting some people still got sick does not weaken it.

example.q:
A supplement maker found that customers who took its new product, VitaBoost, reported fewer colds last winter than customers who did not take it. The company concludes that VitaBoost reduces a person's risk of catching a cold.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?
(A) VitaBoost contains vitamin C, which some studies associate with immune function.
(B) The customers who chose to take VitaBoost were also far more likely than non-takers to wash their hands frequently and get flu vaccinations, habits that independently lower cold risk.
(C) VitaBoost costs more than most competing supplements on the market.
(D) Some customers who took VitaBoost still caught at least one cold during the winter.
(E) The comparison covered only a single winter season.
example.solution: The conclusion is causal: VitaBoost reduces cold risk, inferred from a correlation (takers reported fewer colds). (B) supplies a confounder: the takers were systematically healthier in OTHER ways (handwashing, vaccination) that on their own reduce colds, so their fewer colds may have nothing to do with VitaBoost. That is a direct alternative cause and the strongest weakener. (A) supports the claim, if anything. (C) price is irrelevant to whether it works. (D) misreads the claim — 'reduces risk' does not mean nobody gets a cold, so some takers still catching colds is fully consistent with the conclusion. (E) a single season raises a mild reliability doubt but offers no reason the effect is not real; it is far weaker than naming a concrete confounder. Best answer: (B).
options: (A) VitaBoost contains vitamin C, which some studies associate with immune function.
(B) The customers who chose to take VitaBoost were also far more likely than non-takers to wash their hands frequently and get flu vaccinations, habits that independently lower cold risk.
(C) VitaBoost costs more than most competing supplements on the market.
(D) Some customers who took VitaBoost still caught at least one cold during the winter.
(E) The comparison covered only a single winter season.

traps: Claim-misread: a choice that attacks a stronger claim than the argument actually made ('some still got a cold' vs. 'reduces risk').
Wrong-direction: a choice that quietly supports the conclusion (the vitamin C option).
Vague doubt: 'only one season / small sample' feels critical but does not identify a real flaw and loses to a concrete alternative cause.

subtopics.name: Evaluate the argument
subtopics.summary: You pick the QUESTION whose answer would most help judge whether the argument is sound. The credited choice is a variable that swings the argument both ways: one answer to it strengthens the conclusion, the opposite answer weakens it. It often probes a hidden alternative cause.
keyIdeas: Apply the 'Variance Test': imagine each answer to the question resolved both ways. If one way helps the argument and the other way hurts it, that choice is correct; if the argument is unaffected either way, eliminate it.
The right question usually targets the same gap you would attack to weaken — most often a competing explanation the author ignored.
Reject questions about cost, feasibility, or future plans; they do not test whether the reasoning holds.
Watch for 'baseline already given' traps — a question asking for data the stimulus effectively already supplied.

example.q:
The town of Millbrook installed a roundabout at its busiest intersection last year to cut collisions there. In the year since, collisions at that intersection have fallen 40%. The town's traffic engineer concludes that the roundabout caused the decline and recommends building roundabouts at other intersections.

Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the engineer's conclusion?
(A) Whether the volume of traffic passing through the intersection was about the same in the year after the roundabout as in the year before.
(B) Whether the roundabout was more expensive to build than installing a traffic signal would have been.
(C) Whether drivers find roundabouts more confusing than traffic signals.
(D) How many collisions occurred at that intersection in the years before the roundabout was installed.
(E) Whether the town plans to install roundabouts at intersections that carry light traffic.
example.solution: The conclusion credits the roundabout for the 40% drop in collisions. A key alternative cause is a change in how many cars use the intersection. (A) targets exactly that: if traffic volume stayed the same, fewer collisions points to the roundabout (strengthens); if volume plunged — say a nearby road closed and diverted cars — then fewer collisions could simply reflect fewer cars, not the roundabout (weakens). Because the two possible answers swing the argument in opposite directions, (A) passes the Variance Test. (B) cost and (E) future plans do not bear on whether the roundabout caused the decline. (C) driver opinion does not settle the collision count. (D) looks useful but the '40% fall' already implies the earlier baseline, so it adds little and does not isolate the cause. Best answer: (A).
options: (A) Whether the volume of traffic passing through the intersection was about the same in the year after the roundabout as in the year before.
(B) Whether the roundabout was more expensive to build than installing a traffic signal would have been.
(C) Whether drivers find roundabouts more confusing than traffic signals.
(D) How many collisions occurred at that intersection in the years before the roundabout was installed.
(E) Whether the town plans to install roundabouts at intersections that carry light traffic.

traps: One-directional questions: a choice whose answer helps only if it comes out one way (or never changes the verdict) fails the Variance Test.
Cost/feasibility/future-plan questions that feel relevant to a decision but do not test the argument's logic.
Redundant-baseline questions asking for information the stimulus already provided.

subtopics.name: Inference (must be true)
subtopics.summary: You choose the statement that MUST be true given the facts — a conclusion the passage forces, not merely one it makes plausible. Unlike Strengthen/Weaken, no new outside information is added; the answer stays tightly within what was stated, and conditional logic (if/then) is common.
keyIdeas: The credited answer is provable from the text. If you can imagine the statements true but the choice false, eliminate it.
For conditional stimuli, the safe deductions are the statement itself and its contrapositive (A->B gives not-B->not-A). Reject the converse (B->A) and inverse (not-A->not-B).
Distrust quantity and degree words the passage did not license: 'most', 'all', 'only', 'always' are frequent overreaches.
Prefer the weaker, more cautious choice; the answer is often a modest, almost boring restatement of a locked-in consequence.

example.q:
Every employee at Trenton Manufacturing who received a performance bonus last quarter had also completed the company's advanced safety-training course. No employee in the assembly division completed that course last quarter.

If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
(A) Every employee who completed the safety-training course received a performance bonus.
(B) No employee in the assembly division received a performance bonus last quarter.
(C) Most employees who received a bonus worked outside the assembly division.
(D) The safety-training course was open only to employees outside the assembly division.
(E) Some employees who completed the course did not receive a bonus.
example.solution: Translate: bonus -> completed course. Second fact: every assembly-division employee did NOT complete the course. Apply the contrapositive of the first statement (did not complete course -> no bonus): since no assembly-division employee completed the course, no assembly-division employee received a bonus. That is exactly (B), which must be true. (A) is the converse error — completing the course does not guarantee a bonus. (C) 'most' introduces a quantity the text never gives; we know assembly got zero bonuses, but nothing about the proportions among bonus recipients. (D) confuses 'did not complete' with 'was not allowed to' — the course could have been open to assembly employees who simply did not take it. (E) is unsupported — the facts leave open whether every course-completer got a bonus or not. Only (B) is forced.
options: (A) Every employee who completed the safety-training course received a performance bonus.
(B) No employee in the assembly division received a performance bonus last quarter.
(C) Most employees who received a bonus worked outside the assembly division.
(D) The safety-training course was open only to employees outside the assembly division.
(E) Some employees who completed the course did not receive a bonus.

traps: Converse/inverse of a conditional: swapping or negating the if/then wrongly (the classic (A) here).
Quantity overreach: 'most/all/only' when the passage supports just 'some' or 'none'.
Could-be-true dressed as must-be-true: a plausible statement the facts neither force nor rule out.

subtopics.name: Explain the paradox
subtopics.summary: The stimulus presents two facts that seem to conflict (a surprising result, a discrepancy). You pick the choice that makes BOTH facts true at once by supplying the missing piece. The right answer resolves the tension without contradicting anything stated.
keyIdeas: Pin down the exact two things in tension, then look for a choice that lets both stand simultaneously.
The answer typically introduces an overlooked factor — a change in volume, a difference between the groups, or a hidden cost/benefit — that reconciles them.
Reject choices that deepen the mystery, take a side, or are merely on-topic but do not bridge the two facts.
For revenue/count paradoxes, remember total = per-unit x quantity: a change in one can be swamped by the other.

example.q:
When the city doubled the fee for parking in its downtown garages, officials expected revenue from those garages to rise. Instead, in the year after the increase, total revenue from the downtown garages fell.

Which of the following, if true, most helps to explain the drop in revenue?
(A) The city used the additional per-car fee revenue to fund improvements to public transit.
(B) After the fee increase, the number of cars using the downtown garages fell by more than half.
(C) Parking fees in the neighboring city also rose during the same period.
(D) The downtown garages had been renovated the year before the fee was increased.
(E) Some drivers complained that the new parking fee was too high.
example.solution: The puzzle: the per-car fee doubled, yet total garage revenue FELL. Total revenue = fee per car x number of cars, so a large enough drop in the number of cars can more than offset a higher fee. (B) supplies exactly that: usage fell by more than half, so even at double the fee, total revenue declines (e.g., 100 cars at $10 = $1,000 becomes 40 cars at $20 = $800). Both facts now hold together. (A) concerns how revenue was spent, not why it dropped. (C) a neighboring increase would tend to keep drivers parking downtown, if anything deepening the paradox. (D) prior renovation is irrelevant to the year-over-year revenue fall. (E) complaints alone do not reduce revenue unless drivers actually stopped parking, which the vague statement never establishes; (B) quantifies the behavior change and resolves the discrepancy. Best answer: (B).
options: (A) The city used the additional per-car fee revenue to fund improvements to public transit.
(B) After the fee increase, the number of cars using the downtown garages fell by more than half.
(C) Parking fees in the neighboring city also rose during the same period.
(D) The downtown garages had been renovated the year before the fee was increased.
(E) Some drivers complained that the new parking fee was too high.
