traps: Vague behavior claims ('some complained') that suggest a reason but never establish the change in quantity needed to resolve the numbers.
Deepens-the-paradox choices that would make the surprising result even more puzzling.
On-topic-but-inert facts (how money was spent, prior renovations) that never connect the two conflicting statements.

subtopics.name: Boldface & argument structure
subtopics.summary: Two portions of the passage are printed in boldface and you identify the ROLE each plays (evidence, conclusion, opposing position, counter-consideration, etc.). These appear at higher difficulty on the GMAT Focus Verbal section and reward mapping the argument's skeleton before reading the choices.
keyIdeas: First, find the author's own conclusion and stance; then label each boldface relative to it — does it support the author, oppose the author, or is it a fact the author interprets?
Track the connective words: 'but', 'however', 'yet' usually flip from one side to the author's side; 'because', 'since', 'after all' signal support.
Prescreen answer choices by verbs: rule out any that mislabel a boldface (e.g., calling a piece of evidence 'the conclusion') before weighing fine distinctions.
A boldface can belong to a view the author argues AGAINST — do not assume both boldfaces are on the same side.

example.q:
In the argument below, the two portions in boldface are shown between double asterisks.

For decades, conventional wisdom held that a company's stock price rises when it announces layoffs, because investors reward cost-cutting. **A recent analysis of 500 layoff announcements found that, on average, the announcing companies' share prices declined over the following year.** Some analysts treat this as proof that layoffs destroy shareholder value. But that conclusion may be premature: **many of the companies studied announced layoffs precisely because they were already in financial trouble, which by itself could account for the weak share performance.**

In the argument, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?
(A) The first is the author's main conclusion; the second is evidence offered in support of that conclusion.
(B) The first is a finding that has been used to support a position the author questions; the second is a consideration the author raises to call that position into doubt.
(C) The first is evidence supporting the author's position; the second states that position.
(D) The first states the author's position; the second is an objection that the author goes on to reject.
(E) The first and the second each provide independent support for the same conclusion.
example.solution: Map the argument first. The author's stance appears after 'But that conclusion may be premature' — the author DOUBTS the claim that layoffs destroy shareholder value. Boldface 1 is the analysis finding (share prices fell); 'some analysts' use it to support the claim the author questions, so it is evidence for a position the author disputes. Boldface 2 is the author's counter — an alternative explanation (the firms were already troubled) offered to undercut that position. That matches (B) exactly. (A) and (C) wrongly call one of the boldfaces the author's conclusion or evidence for it, but boldface 1 supports the OPPOSING view and boldface 2 is a doubt, not the author's stated main conclusion. (D) reverses the roles — the first is not the author's position, and the author does not reject the second, he endorses it. (E) is wrong because the two boldfaces support opposing sides, not the same conclusion. Best answer: (B).
options: (A) The first is the author's main conclusion; the second is evidence offered in support of that conclusion.
(B) The first is a finding that has been used to support a position the author questions; the second is a consideration the author raises to call that position into doubt.
(C) The first is evidence supporting the author's position; the second states that position.
(D) The first states the author's position; the second is an objection that the author goes on to reject.
(E) The first and the second each provide independent support for the same conclusion.

traps: Mislabeling evidence as the conclusion (or vice versa) — the fastest way to fall for (A)/(C).
Assuming both boldfaces defend the same side when one actually belongs to a view the author opposes.
Reversed-role choices that swap which boldface the author supports and which he doubts.

subtopics.name: Flawed reasoning
subtopics.summary: You identify the logical flaw that makes an argument vulnerable — the question usually reads 'the argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it...'. The answer names a reasoning error (attacking the source, confusing correlation with cause, overlooking an alternative, unwarranted generalization) in abstract terms.
keyIdeas: Name the fallacy in your own words before reading choices, then match: common ones are ad hominem (attacking the arguer), causation-from-correlation, sampling/generalization errors, and equivocation.
The answer must describe the ARGUER's reasoning error, not simply state a true criticism of the underlying topic.
Distinguish 'dismissing a claim' from 'asserting the opposite claim' — a choice that overstates what the arguer concluded is a trap.
Abstract answer choices are the norm; test each against what the argument actually did, discarding any flaw the argument did not commit.

example.q:
A newspaper columnist wrote: 'The mayor claims that her new economic policies caused the city's unemployment rate to fall last year. But the mayor is a politician seeking reelection, so her claim that the policies helped should be dismissed.'

The columnist's argument is most vulnerable to criticism on the grounds that it
(A) rejects a claim merely because of the motives or position of the person who made it, rather than addressing the evidence for the claim.
(B) fails to consider that a city's unemployment rate can be affected by national economic conditions.
(C) assumes without justification that the mayor's policies had no effect on unemployment.
(D) relies on unemployment statistics that may have been gathered improperly.
(E) treats an effect as though it were a cause.
example.solution: The columnist dismisses the mayor's claim solely because she is a politician who wants reelection — an attack on the source rather than on the claim's merits (the ad hominem fallacy). (A) describes exactly that error, so it is the credited answer. (B) is a legitimate real-world point about unemployment, but it criticizes the mayor's claim, not the columnist's reasoning, which is what the question asks about. (C) overstates the columnist's conclusion: he says the claim should be DISMISSED, which is not the same as asserting the policies had NO effect — dismissing a claim for lack of credibility is different from proving the opposite. (D) no issue with how the statistics were gathered is raised in the argument. (E) there is no reversal of cause and effect in the columnist's reasoning. Best answer: (A).
options: (A) rejects a claim merely because of the motives or position of the person who made it, rather than addressing the evidence for the claim.
(B) fails to consider that a city's unemployment rate can be affected by national economic conditions.
(C) assumes without justification that the mayor's policies had no effect on unemployment.
(D) relies on unemployment statistics that may have been gathered improperly.
(E) treats an effect as though it were a cause.

traps: True-but-off-target: a valid criticism of the topic (national economy) that is not the arguer's logical flaw.
Overstated conclusion: treating 'the claim should be dismissed' as 'the opposite is proven' (choice C).
Naming a fallacy the argument never commits (bad data, cause/effect reversal) just because it sounds like standard CR language.

sections.name: Data Insights
sections.format: Five formats mixing quant, verbal & data literacy. Now weighed EQUALLY into the total score — the signature Focus Edition section.

subtopics.name: The answer-choice framework
subtopics.summary: Every Data Sufficiency question in the Data Insights section uses the same five fixed answer choices (A-E); your only job is to decide whether the statements give ENOUGH information to answer the stem, never to compute the final answer. Owning the fixed choices and a disciplined order of testing is the whole game.
formulas: A = (1) alone sufficient, (2) alone not; B = (2) alone sufficient, (1) alone not; C = both together sufficient, neither alone; D = each alone sufficient; E = not sufficient even together.
AD / BCE decision tree: if (1) is sufficient, the answer is A or D; if (1) is not, the answer is B, C, or E.
keyIdeas: Memorize the choices cold so you spend zero time re-reading them: A and B are 'one alone', C is 'need both', D is 'either alone', E is 'neither, even together'.
Use the AD/BCE grid: test (1) first. Sufficient -> A or D. Not sufficient -> B, C, or E. Then test (2) to land on the exact letter mechanically.
Evaluate each statement in complete isolation: never let a fact from statement (1) carry into your test of statement (2).
You only need to know that a unique value (or a definite yes/no) EXISTS - you do not need to find it. Stop the instant sufficiency is decided.

example.q: What is the value of x? (1) 3x - 7 = 11 (2) x^2 - 8x + 12 = 0
example.solution: Statement (1): 3x - 7 = 11 -> 3x = 18 -> x = 6, exactly one value -> SUFFICIENT (so the answer is A or D). Statement (2): x^2 - 8x + 12 = 0 -> (x - 2)(x - 6) = 0 -> x = 2 or x = 6, two possible values -> NOT sufficient. (1) sufficient, (2) not -> answer A.

traps: Seeing statement (1) work and picking D without ever testing (2) alone (or the reverse).
Mixing up A and B: statement (1) is always the first statement listed - keep the order straight.
Treating 'has an equation' as 'is sufficient': a quadratic usually yields two solutions and is often insufficient for a value question.

subtopics.name: Value questions
subtopics.summary: The stem asks for a specific quantity ('What is the value of ...?'). A statement is sufficient ONLY if it narrows the target to exactly one possible value; if two or more legal values survive, it is insufficient.
formulas: Average (arithmetic mean) = (sum of terms) / (number of terms), so knowing the sum alone is enough for the mean.
To pin n unknowns you generally need n independent linear equations; a single quadratic or |absolute-value| equation often leaves 2 candidate values.
keyIdeas: Solve for the EXACT expression the stem asks for - you can often determine x + y (or a ratio) without ever finding x and y individually.
One value = sufficient; a range or two-plus values = insufficient. Prove insufficiency by producing two different legal values.
Mine the stem for hidden constraints (positive, integer, nonzero) that can collapse two candidates down to one and flip a statement to sufficient.

example.q: What is the value of x + y? (1) 2x + 2y = 20 (2) x - y = 4
example.solution: Statement (1): 2x + 2y = 20 -> divide by 2 -> x + y = 10. The stem asks only for x + y, which is fixed at 10 even though x and y individually are unknown -> SUFFICIENT (answer A or D). Statement (2): x - y = 4 gives no handle on x + y (e.g. x=5, y=1 -> x+y=6; x=6, y=2 -> x+y=8) -> NOT sufficient. (1) sufficient, (2) not -> answer A.

traps: Rejecting statement (1) because you 'can't find x and y separately' - you don't need them, only the requested combination x + y.
Counting a quadratic or an absolute-value equation as one value when it actually delivers two.
Assuming two unknowns always require two equations; a single proportional equation can hand you the requested combined value alone.

subtopics.name: Yes/No questions
subtopics.summary: The stem asks a yes/no question ('Is n even?', 'Is x > 0?'). A statement is sufficient when it forces a single definite answer for EVERY allowed case - always YES or always NO. A definite NO is exactly as sufficient as a definite YES; only 'sometimes yes, sometimes no' is insufficient.
formulas: Odd power keeps the sign: x^3 > 0 <=> x > 0; even power hides it: x^2 > 0 only tells you x != 0.
even x anything = even; odd x odd = odd; even + even = even, odd + odd = even, even + odd = odd.
keyIdeas: Sufficiency means a CONSISTENT answer, not a positive one - a statement that guarantees 'No' every time is fully sufficient.
Run the two-case test: try to make the answer YES with one legal value and NO with another. If you succeed, that statement is insufficient.
Plug in strategic numbers - 0, 1, a fraction, and a negative - to expose statements that only behave for 'nice' positive integers.

example.solution: Statement (1): x^2 > 25 -> x > 5 OR x < -5. So x could be 6 (Yes) or -6 (No) -> sometimes yes, sometimes no -> NOT sufficient. Statement (2): x < 2 means x can never exceed 5, so the answer is a definite 'No' in every case -> a consistent answer -> SUFFICIENT. (1) not sufficient, (2) sufficient -> answer B.

traps: Thinking a statement that always yields 'No' is insufficient - a consistent NO answers the question just as well as YES.
Forgetting the negative branch of a square root or even power (x^2 > 25 also allows x < -5).
Testing only positive integers and missing the fraction or negative that flips the answer.

subtopics.name: C-trap & sufficiency pitfalls
subtopics.summary: The C-trap is the single most common DS mistake: you pick C (both statements together) because combining feels safe, when one statement alone was already sufficient (A, B, or D) - or when even together they stay insufficient (E). The cure is disciplined isolation: fully test each statement alone before you ever combine.
keyIdeas: Finish the question 'is (1) alone enough?' and then 'is (2) alone enough?' BEFORE combining - combining is the last resort, not the default move.
Choose C only after proving BOTH that neither statement alone works AND that together they pin a unique value or definite yes/no.
Watch the mirror E-trap: two insufficient statements combined are not automatically sufficient - confirm that together they eliminate every other case.
A statement can look 'incomplete' yet already be sufficient; seemingly-needed information in the other statement is often just bait for C.

example.q: What is the two-digit positive integer n? (1) The sum of the digits of n is 9. (2) n is a multiple of 9 between 40 and 50.
example.solution: Statement (1): digit sum 9 -> n could be 18, 27, 36, 45, 54, 63, 72, 81, or 90 -> many values -> NOT sufficient. Statement (2): multiples of 9 between 40 and 50 -> only 45 -> exactly one value -> SUFFICIENT ALONE. Since (2) already fixes n = 45, statement (1) adds nothing you actually need. (1) not sufficient, (2) sufficient -> answer B. The C-trap is to pair the 'digit-sum' clue with the 'range' clue and pick C, but statement (2) stands on its own.

traps: Defaulting to C because using both statements 'obviously' answers the question, without ever checking each one alone (the classic C-trap).
Picking E when the combined statements do resolve it, or picking C when combined statements still leave two cases (should be E).
Letting a fact from statement (1) leak into your evaluation of statement (2), making (2) look sufficient when by itself it is not.

subtopics.name: Navigating multiple sources/tabs
subtopics.summary: Every Multi-Source Reasoning set opens with 2-3 tabbed sources (emails, memos, tables, charts) that you switch between; this skill is finding WHERE each fact lives before you start computing. On test day one MSR set drives roughly 3 of the 20 Data Insights questions, so fast, accurate tab navigation is the gate to all of them.
keyIdeas: First pass: read all tabs once to build a mental index of what each tab holds (labels, units, dates) — do NOT try to answer yet.
Note which tab carries which VARIABLE (sales vs. staffing vs. dates); most trap answers are the right number pulled from the wrong tab.
Many questions are a two-step lookup: use tab A to select an item, then read tab B for the attribute you actually need.

example.q: MSR set with three tabs. Tab 1 [Q2 Sales, $]: Store A 120,000; Store B 90,000; Store C 150,000. Tab 2 [Staffing, salespeople]: Store A 6; Store B 5; Store C 8. Tab 3 [Memo]: 'Store C launches a new product line in September.' Question: How many salespeople work at the store that had the highest Q2 sales? (A) 5 (B) 6 (C) 8 (D) 15 (E) 150,000
example.solution: Two-step, two-tab lookup. Step 1 — on Tab 1 the highest Q2 sales is Store C at 150,000. Step 2 — cross to Tab 2 and read Store C's staffing: 8. Answer (C). No arithmetic is needed; the whole task is knowing that 'sales' lives on Tab 1 and 'salespeople' lives on Tab 2.

traps: Pulling the number from the store you happened to read first, or grabbing the largest staffing figure (8) or largest sales figure without cross-referencing.
Answer (E) 150,000 baits anyone who forgets the question asked for people, not dollars — a wrong-tab/wrong-unit answer.

subtopics.name: Cross-source inference
subtopics.summary: The heart of MSR: draw a conclusion that no single tab states, by combining a rule from one source with data split across the others. The GMAT rewards the reader who realizes eligibility, cause, or a category depends on facts that are deliberately scattered across tabs.
keyIdeas: Locate the governing RULE or definition first (often an email/memo tab), then check each candidate against every clause of that rule.
A conclusion is only supported if ALL required conditions are met using data from the relevant tabs — one failed clause disqualifies a candidate.
Beware 'partial match' items that satisfy the condition you can see on one tab but fail a condition hidden on another.

example.q: MSR set with three tabs. Tab 1 [Grant policy]: A proposal is funded only if BOTH (a) its budget is under $50,000 AND (b) its lead researcher has at least 5 years of postdoctoral experience. Tab 2 [Proposals]: P — budget $45,000, lead Dr. Lee; Q — budget $60,000, lead Dr. Ortiz; R — budget $30,000, lead Dr. Chen. Tab 3 [Researchers, postdoc years]: Dr. Lee 3; Dr. Ortiz 8; Dr. Chen 6. Question: Which proposal(s) qualify for funding? (A) P only (B) R only (C) P and R (D) Q and R (E) None
example.solution: Apply both clauses of the Tab 1 rule using Tab 2 (budget) and Tab 3 (experience). P: budget $45k < $50k passes (a), but Dr. Lee has 3 years < 5 — fails (b). Q: budget $60k is not under $50k — fails (a) immediately. R: budget $30k < $50k passes (a), and Dr. Chen has 6 years ≥ 5 — passes (b). Only R meets both conditions. Answer (B). The verdict on R exists in no single tab: it requires the rule, the budget, and the experience together.

traps: Choosing P because its budget alone looks fine — you must also check the experience tab, where it fails.
Answer (C) P and R rewards checking only the budget clause and ignoring clause (b) entirely.

subtopics.name: Quantitative synthesis
subtopics.summary: MSR questions that require gathering numbers from two or three tabs and running one calculation (totals, profit, percent change, per-unit rates). The math is deliberately simple; the difficulty is collecting every relevant figure — including a cost or adjustment buried on a separate tab.
formulas: Profit = (Price − Unit cost) × Units − Fixed costs
Percent change = (New − Old) / Old × 100
Weighted total = Σ (value_i × count_i)
keyIdeas: List every number the formula needs, then hunt each one across the tabs before computing — the last input is often on the tab you'd skip.
Watch for a one-off adjustment (flat fee, shipping, tax, a single-region rule) that applies to only part of the data.
Keep units consistent (per-unit vs. total, monthly vs. annual) when values come from different sources.

example.q: MSR set with three tabs. Tab 1 [Pricing]: the product sells for $80 per unit; unit cost is $50. Tab 2 [Units sold]: East region 2,000 units; West region 1,500 units. Tab 3 [Logistics note]: a flat $10,000 shipping charge applies to the East region only; West ships free. Question: What is the company's total profit across both regions? (A) $45,000 (B) $50,000 (C) $85,000 (D) $95,000 (E) $105,000
example.solution: Margin per unit = $80 − $50 = $30 (Tab 1). East gross = 2,000 × $30 = $60,000, minus the $10,000 East-only shipping from Tab 3 = $50,000. West gross = 1,500 × $30 = $45,000, no shipping. Total profit = $50,000 + $45,000 = $95,000. Answer (D).

traps: Answer (E) $105,000 is the total profit if you forget the $10,000 shipping charge on Tab 3 — the classic 'missed a tab' trap.
Applying the shipping charge to both regions (or per-unit instead of as a flat fee) yields other wrong values engineered into the options.

subtopics.name: True/False & yes-no sets
subtopics.summary: The most common MSR question shape: three separate statements, each answered Yes/No (or True/False), and scored all-or-nothing — you must get all three correct to earn the point. Each statement is evaluated independently against a definition on one tab plus data on the others.
formulas: Threshold test: benefit if metric > cutoff (e.g., Net benefit = discount rate × spend − annual fee; 'worth it' when > 0)
keyIdeas: Treat each statement as its own mini-question — a Yes on one tells you nothing about the others.
Anchor to the exact wording: 'worth it', 'at least', 'exceeds' each set a precise threshold you test against the tab data.
Because it is all-or-nothing, double-check the statement you feel most sure about — one careless miss costs the whole item.

example.q: MSR set with three tabs. Tab 1 [Membership tiers]: Silver — $100 annual fee, 5% purchase discount; Gold — $250 fee, 12% discount; Platinum — $500 fee, 20% discount. Tab 2 [Annual spend]: Customer 1 spends $2,000; Customer 2 spends $3,000; Customer 3 spends $1,000. Tab 3 [Rule]: a tier is 'worth it' for a customer only if the discount it saves that year exceeds its annual fee. For each statement, select Yes or No. (S1) For Customer 1, Gold membership is worth it. (S2) For Customer 2, Gold membership is worth it. (S3) For Customer 3, Silver membership is worth it.
example.solution: Apply the Tab 3 rule (discount saved > fee) with fees from Tab 1 and spend from Tab 2. S1: Gold saves 12% × $2,000 = $240, versus the $250 fee; $240 < $250, so NOT worth it → No. S2: Gold saves 12% × $3,000 = $360, versus $250; $360 > $250 → Yes. S3: Silver saves 5% × $1,000 = $50, versus the $100 fee; $50 < $100 → No. Answers: S1 No, S2 Yes, S3 No.

traps: Assuming a higher discount rate always wins — S1 fails because the 12% Gold discount ($240) doesn't clear the $250 fee, a near-miss engineered to catch quick eyeballing.
Carrying a Yes/No verdict from one customer to another; each statement uses different spend and must be recomputed.
Forgetting the all-or-nothing scoring and skipping a full check on the statement that 'looks obvious'.

subtopics.name: Sorting & reading tables
subtopics.summary: The core Table Analysis skill: a small spreadsheet-style table with a sort drop-down is shown, and you re-sort by a chosen column to check position/threshold claims. Every TA item on the GMAT gives three True/False (or Yes/No) statements and awards credit only if all three are answered correctly.
keyIdeas: Actually use the sort drop-down on the exact column a statement names rather than eyeballing the given order — misreading row order is the number-one error.
Read each statement literally: words like 'third row', 'more than', 'at least', 'highest' define exactly what makes it true.
Evaluate the three statements independently; there is no partial credit, so re-check each before submitting.

example.q:
Table Analysis. A sortable table (re-sort by any column with the drop-down) lists six regional sales offices. Columns: Office | Units Sold | Revenue ($000) | Employees.
Austin, 420, 1,260, 14
Boston, 310, 1,085, 10
Denver, 500, 1,400, 20
Fresno, 280, 700, 8
Miami, 360, 1,170, 12
Seattle, 450, 1,575, 15
For each statement select True or False.
(1) When the table is sorted by Units Sold from highest to lowest, Seattle is in the third row.
(2) The office with the highest Revenue also employs the most people.
(3) When the table is sorted by Revenue from lowest to highest, the office in the first row sold fewer than 300 units.
example.solution: (1) Sort Units Sold descending: Denver 500, Seattle 450, Austin 420, Miami 360, Boston 310, Fresno 280. Seattle is 2nd, not 3rd -> FALSE. (2) Highest Revenue = Seattle ($1,575k); most Employees = Denver (20). Different offices -> FALSE. (3) Sort Revenue ascending: Fresno 700, Boston 1,085, Miami 1,170, Austin 1,260, Denver 1,400, Seattle 1,575. First row = Fresno, which sold 280 units < 300 -> TRUE. Answers: False, False, True.

traps: Confusing ascending with descending: after a highest-to-lowest sort the first row holds the maximum, not the minimum.
Assuming the leader in one column also leads another (highest revenue is not necessarily the most employees).

subtopics.name: Descriptive statistics from tables
subtopics.summary: A frequent Table Analysis twist: statements ask you to compare the median, mean, or range of a numeric column. The GMAT loves the median because sorting the column makes the middle value instant, while the mean requires actually summing.
formulas: mean = (sum of the values) / n
median = the middle value when the column is sorted (odd n); the average of the two middle values (even n)
range = maximum value - minimum value
keyIdeas: Sort the relevant column first; for an odd count the median is simply the middle row, needing no arithmetic.
For 'mean vs median' you often don't need the exact mean — a few large high-end values pull the mean above the median (right skew) and vice versa.
Compute only what a statement asks: once the column is sorted, range = top value minus bottom value is immediate.

example.q:
Table Analysis. A sortable table lists seven employees. Columns: Employee | Age | Salary ($000) | Years of Service.
A, 28, 52, 3
B, 34, 61, 6
C, 41, 70, 12
D, 45, 88, 15
E, 52, 95, 20
F, 38, 66, 9
G, 30, 58, 4
For each statement select True or False.
(1) The median Age of the seven employees is greater than their mean Age.
(2) The mean Salary is greater than the median Salary.
(3) The range of Years of Service is more than twice the median Years of Service.
example.solution: (1) Ages sorted: 28, 30, 34, 38, 41, 45, 52 -> median = 38. Mean = 268/7 = 38.3. 38 is not greater than 38.3 -> FALSE. (2) Salaries sorted: 52, 58, 61, 66, 70, 88, 95 -> median = 66. Mean = 490/7 = 70. 70 > 66 -> TRUE. (3) Years sorted: 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 15, 20 -> median = 9, range = 20 - 3 = 17. Twice the median = 18; 17 is not more than 18 -> FALSE. Answers: False, True, False.

traps: Reading the median off the unsorted table order — you must sort the column first or the 'middle' value is meaningless.
Assuming mean equals median: a nearly symmetric column can still have the mean sitting just above or below the median, so verify rather than assume.

subtopics.name: Ratios & proportions in tables
subtopics.summary: Table Analysis statements routinely require a derived quantity — a percentage, a per-unit figure, a profit margin, or the ratio between two columns — computed for specific rows or for column totals. The arithmetic is light; the difficulty is choosing the right rows and the right base.
formulas: percentage = (part / whole) x 100
per-unit value = total / quantity
profit margin = (Revenue - Cost) / Revenue
a ratio a:b is compared to a threshold by evaluating a/b as a decimal
keyIdeas: Build the derived quantity (margin, per-unit, share) only for the rows a statement actually compares, not for the whole table.
For a 'ratio of totals' statement, sum each column first and then divide — do not average the per-row ratios.
Convert a target ratio to a decimal so you can compare directly (3:4 = 0.75).

example.q:
Table Analysis. A sortable table lists five product lines. Columns: Product | Revenue ($M) | Cost ($M) | Units Sold (thousands).
P, 120, 90, 40
Q, 80, 50, 25
R, 150, 120, 60
S, 60, 36, 15
T, 200, 130, 50
For each statement select True or False.
(1) Product S has the highest profit margin, where profit margin = (Revenue - Cost) / Revenue.
(2) For the five products combined, the ratio of total Cost to total Revenue is less than 3 : 4.
(3) Product R earns more revenue per unit sold than Product Q.
example.solution: (1) Margins: P = 30/120 = 25%; Q = 30/80 = 37.5%; R = 30/150 = 20%; S = 24/60 = 40%; T = 70/200 = 35%. S at 40% is the highest -> TRUE. (2) Total Revenue = 120+80+150+60+200 = 610; total Cost = 90+50+120+36+130 = 426. 426/610 = 0.70; 3:4 = 0.75. 0.70 < 0.75 -> TRUE. (3) Revenue per unit (Revenue in $M / Units in thousands gives $ per unit): R = 150/60 = $2,500; Q = 80/25 = $3,200. R < Q -> FALSE. Answers: True, True, False.

traps: Averaging the per-row percentages instead of using totals: total cost / total revenue is not the mean of the individual margins.
Mismatched units: with Revenue in $M and Units in thousands the per-unit figure lands in dollars, so keep the scale straight before comparing.

subtopics.name: Reading charts & graphs
subtopics.summary: Graphics Interpretation shows a bar chart, line graph, pie chart, histogram, or bubble chart and asks you to complete sentences from drop-down menus by reading values off the graphic. The GMAT rarely wants a pixel-perfect reading — it tests whether you can compute rates, percent changes, totals, and comparisons from what the axes, legend, and units actually say.
formulas: Percent change = (new − old) / old × 100%
Rate of change (slope) between two points = (y₂ − y₁) / (x₂ − x₁)
Average (mean) = sum of values / number of values
Percent of total = (part / whole) × 100%
keyIdeas: Read the axis titles, units, and scale FIRST (thousands? percent? does the axis start at zero?) before reading any value — most GI errors are unit or scale misreads, not arithmetic.
For 'greatest increase/decrease' blanks, first decide whether it asks for absolute change (Δ in units) or percentage change — they frequently point to different bars.
Estimate to the nearest gridline rather than obsessing over exact pixels: the drop-down options are spaced far enough apart that a clean read picks the answer.

example.q: A line graph shows one company's quarterly revenue (in $ millions): Q1 = 20, Q2 = 25, Q3 = 45, Q4 = 75. Complete the statements. (a) The quarter with the greatest percentage increase over the previous quarter was [Q2 / Q3 / Q4]. (b) That percentage increase was closest to [25% / 67% / 80% / 125%].
example.solution: Compute each quarter-over-quarter percent change. Q2: (25 − 20)/20 = 25%. Q3: (45 − 25)/25 = 80%. Q4: (75 − 45)/45 ≈ 66.7%. The largest is Q3 at 80%, even though Q4 has the biggest raw jump (+$30M vs Q3's +$20M). Blank (a) = Q3; blank (b) = 80%.

traps: Confusing the largest absolute jump (Q4, +$30M) with the largest percentage jump (Q3, +80%) — the credited blank asks for percent.
Overlooking that the y-axis is in millions, or misreading a broken / non-zero baseline that visually exaggerates the differences between bars.

subtopics.name: Scatterplots & correlation
subtopics.summary: The single most common Graphics Interpretation graphic: a scatterplot of paired data, usually with a line of best fit (trend/regression line). Drop-downs ask you to describe the direction and strength of the correlation and to read or predict a value from the trend line.
formulas: Correlation coefficient r lies in [−1, +1]; the sign of r equals the direction of the best-fit line's slope.
|r| near 1 = strong (points hug the line); |r| near 0 = weak or no correlation (points scattered).
Predicted value from the line of best fit: ŷ = m·x + b, with m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁) taken from two clear points ON the line.
Positive correlation: y tends to rise as x rises; negative correlation: y tends to fall as x rises.
keyIdeas: Read direction from the slope of the point cloud / best-fit line (up-to-the-right = positive, down-to-the-right = negative) and strength from how tightly the points cluster around it.
To predict a value, read two clean points off the LINE (not off a scattered dot), compute the slope, and plug in your x — don't eyeball a single dot near that x.
Correlation is not causation, and the trend only holds inside the plotted range; be suspicious of blanks that ask you to extrapolate beyond the data.

example.q: A scatterplot plots 12 cities' average annual temperature (°C) on the x-axis (5 to 30) against annual household heating cost ($) on the y-axis (200 to 1,600). The points form a tight downward-sloping band, and a line of best fit passes through the points (5, 1,400) and (30, 400). Complete the statements. (a) The correlation between temperature and heating cost is best described as [strong positive / weak positive / strong negative / no correlation]. (b) Based on the line of best fit, a city averaging 20 °C has a predicted heating cost closest to [$600 / $700 / $800 / $900].
example.solution: The band slopes down to the right and the points hug the line, so the correlation is strong negative (blank a). Slope of the best-fit line = (400 − 1,400)/(30 − 5) = −1,000/25 = −40 $ per °C. At x = 20: ŷ = 1,400 + (−40)(20 − 5) = 1,400 − 600 = $800 (blank b).

traps: Calling a tight downward band 'weak' because the dollar values are large, or mislabeling the sign — a downward slope is NEGATIVE correlation regardless of magnitude.
Predicting off a nearby scattered dot instead of the best-fit line, or extrapolating the line outside the plotted 5–30 °C range.

subtopics.name: Drop-down completion logic
subtopics.summary: Every Graphics Interpretation item is a sentence (usually one or two of them) with drop-down blanks you fill from fixed menus. This subtopic is the format mechanic: parse the sentence's logic, answer each blank from the graphic, and remember the item is all-or-nothing — every blank must be correct for the question to count, with no partial credit.
formulas: Percent of whole = (part / whole) × 100%
Difference of two categories = value_A − value_B (read each off the graphic, then subtract)
keyIdeas: Treat each blank as its own read of the graph, but keep the sentence internally consistent — if blank 1 is a percentage and blank 2 is 'that amount in dollars,' blank 2 must match the CORRECT blank 1, not your first guess.
Plug each drop-down option back into the sentence and test it against the graphic; wrong options are usually real numbers from the chart that answer a different question.
Because both blanks must be right for any credit, verify the 'easy' blank too before you commit — a careless second blank sinks a correctly reasoned first one.

example.q: A pie chart divides a $200,000 marketing budget: Digital 40%, TV 25%, Print 15%, Events 12%, Other 8%. Complete the statement: 'The two largest categories together account for [50% / 55% / 65% / 80%] of the budget, which is [$100,000 / $110,000 / $130,000 / $160,000].'
example.solution: The two largest slices are Digital (40%) and TV (25%), summing to 65% → blank 1 = 65%. Then 65% × $200,000 = $130,000 → blank 2 = $130,000. Blank 2 must be consistent with the correct blank 1: the trap value $100,000 is 50% of the budget, which pairs only with a wrong first answer.

traps: Picking a dollar value that is consistent with a WRONG percentage (50% → $100,000) — since both blanks are scored together, they must agree with each other and with the chart.
Adding the wrong two slices — 'largest' means largest by value (Digital 40% + TV 25% = 65%), not two categories that merely look adjacent on the chart (e.g., Digital 40% + Print 15% = 55%).

subtopics.name: Quantitative two-part
subtopics.summary: The most common Two-Part Analysis flavor: a word problem with two unknowns (often a system of equations, a rate/mixture, or a ratio) presented as a two-column table where you pick one value per column from a shared option list. The two columns are usually the two quantities you must solve for (e.g., 'number of adult tickets' and 'number of child tickets'), and only one combination satisfies all the given information.
formulas: Two linear equations in two unknowns: solve by substitution or elimination.
If a + b = S and pa + qb = T, then b = (T - pS)/(q - p) and a = S - b.
Always sanity-check the pair against BOTH original equations before selecting.
keyIdeas: Translate the prompt into two equations, solve once, then map each solved value to the correct column heading — do not re-derive per column.
Because you select independently in each column, the answer set is a single ordered pair; find it algebraically rather than testing every option.
Note the column labels precisely (which price/rate/group each column refers to) — that mapping is where most errors happen.
If algebra is messy, back-solve: plug candidate values from the shared list into the two conditions.

example.q:
At a fundraiser, adult tickets cost $12 each and child tickets cost $5 each. A total of 30 tickets were sold for total revenue of $248. In the table, select the number of Adult tickets sold and the number of Child tickets sold, consistent with this information. Make exactly one selection in each column; the same option list is used for both columns.
Column 1 — Adult tickets sold. Column 2 — Child tickets sold.
Shared options: 8, 12, 14, 16, 18, 22.
example.solution: Let a = adult tickets, c = child tickets. Then a + c = 30 and 12a + 5c = 248. Substitute c = 30 - a: 12a + 5(30 - a) = 248 -> 12a + 150 - 5a = 248 -> 7a = 98 -> a = 14, so c = 16. Check: 12(14) + 5(16) = 168 + 80 = 248 and 14 + 16 = 30 ✓. Column 1 (Adult) = 14; Column 2 (Child) = 16.

traps: Column-swap trap: if you accidentally solve 5a + 12c = 248 (prices reversed) you get a = 16, c = 14 — both values are on the list, so 16/14 is engineered to look right if you mislabel the columns.
Distractor values (e.g., 8, 12, 18, 22) satisfy only ONE of the two equations — a value that gives the right total but wrong revenue passes a lazy check.
Nothing forbids picking the same number in both columns; do not assume the two answers must differ.

subtopics.name: Verbal & logical two-part
subtopics.summary: A logic/reading version of Two-Part Analysis: given a short argument or a two-sided debate, you select one statement per column from a shared list, where the columns are opposing or complementary roles (e.g., 'most strengthens Position A' vs 'most strengthens Position B', or 'conclusion' vs 'supporting premise'). It is Critical-Reasoning logic delivered in the two-column format — no grammar or Sentence Correction.
keyIdeas: Read the two column headings first and label each as a direction (support / oppose / assume / conclude) before touching the options.
For each option decide which side it helps, if any — most items are irrelevant filler designed to eat time; discard them fast.
Pick the SINGLE strongest fit per column: prefer options that are decisive and specific over ones that are hedged ('some', 'mild') or merely about the company's general track record.
The two answers are independent selections, so an option that could serve one column should be tested against the other heading too.

example.q:
Two analysts disagree about whether Company X should launch Product Y. Analyst 1 argues it should; Analyst 2 argues it should not. Using the shared list below, select one statement that, if true, most strongly supports LAUNCHING Product Y (Column 1) and one statement that, if true, most strongly supports NOT launching Product Y (Column 2). Make exactly one selection in each column.
Shared options:
(A) Independent market research shows that demand for Product Y's category is growing rapidly.
(B) A well-funded competitor is about to release a product that industry experts consider clearly superior to Product Y.
(C) Company X has successfully launched several products over the past decade.
(D) Product Y will be sold in the same stores that already carry Company X's current products.
(E) A few consumers have expressed mild interest in Product Y.
(F) Company X's current products are profitable.
Column 1 — Most strongly supports launching. Column 2 — Most strongly supports NOT launching.
example.solution: Column 1 = (A): rapidly growing category demand is a direct, specific reason the launch would succeed. (C) is about the company's past record, not Product Y's prospects; (E) is too weak ('a few', 'mild'); (D) and (F) are neutral background — so (A) is the strongest pro-launch pick. Column 2 = (B): a clearly superior competing product about to capture the same market is a decisive reason the launch would fail. No other option argues against launching. Column 1 = (A); Column 2 = (B).

traps: Track-record trap: (C) 'has launched products successfully before' feels supportive but says nothing about THIS product's market — it is a classic wrong pick for Column 1.
Strength trap: hedged options like (E) ('a few consumers, mild interest') are deliberately weaker than the credited answer; the GMAT rewards the more decisive statement.
Neutral filler (D, F) is included only to consume reading time — an option that is merely true and on-topic is not the same as one that supports the stated side.

subtopics.name: Optimization & trade-off
subtopics.summary: A Two-Part Analysis type where a budget or total constraint links two quantities, and the two columns ask for extreme values (e.g., 'maximum number of A' and 'maximum number of B') or a max/min pair. The core idea is the trade-off: to maximize one item you must minimize the other, subject to floor/ceiling constraints like 'at least k of each'.
formulas: To maximize one quantity under a shared budget/total, set every other quantity to its minimum allowed value.
Max whole units of an item costing p within remaining budget R: floor(R / p).
Remaining budget after fixing the other item = Total budget − (min count of other item × its price).
keyIdeas: Optimize each column separately: for 'max of one', push the other to its smallest legal value, then spend the leftover.
Respect ALL constraints (minimum counts, integer counts, total budget) — the binding constraint is usually the 'at least k of each' floor, not just the budget.
Take the floor for whole items; leftover budget that can't buy a full extra unit is simply unused.
Re-verify the extreme pair satisfies every constraint at once — an answer that maxes one column may violate a minimum on the other.

example.q:
A researcher has a budget of $600 to buy two types of equipment: type A costs $40 each and type B costs $90 each. She must buy at least 3 of each type, and equipment is bought only in whole units. Using the shared list, select the greatest possible number of type A she can buy (Column 1) and the greatest possible number of type B she can buy (Column 2). Each column is optimized on its own. Make exactly one selection in each column.
Shared options: 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8.
Column 1 — Max type A. Column 2 — Max type B.
example.solution: Max type A (Column 1): minimize B at its floor, B = 3, costing 3 × 90 = 270. Remaining = 600 − 270 = 330. floor(330 / 40) = 8 (8 × 40 = 320 ≤ 330; 9 × 40 = 360 > 330). So max A = 8, and A = 8 ≥ 3 ✓. Max type B (Column 2): minimize A at its floor, A = 3, costing 3 × 40 = 120. Remaining = 600 − 120 = 480. floor(480 / 90) = 5 (5 × 90 = 450 ≤ 480; 6 × 90 = 540 > 480). So max B = 5, and B = 5 ≥ 3 ✓. Column 1 = 8; Column 2 = 5.

traps: Ignoring the 'at least 3 of each' floor: dropping the other item to 0 gives max B = floor(600/90) = 6 — that is why 6 is on the list, but B = 6 forces A ≤ 1, violating A ≥ 3, so 6 is the trap answer.
Rounding up instead of taking the floor: leftover cash that can't buy a whole extra unit does not add one.
Optimizing both columns simultaneously — the columns are independent extremes, not a single feasible purchase, so don't try to make one basket satisfy both maxes at once.

scoring.note: Focus Edition total is 205-805 in 10-point steps; each section (QR, VR, DI) is scored 60-90 and the three weigh EQUALLY into the total. Focus scores are NOT directly comparable to old-GMAT 200-800 numbers — use GMAC's official concordance. Total percentiles are from the official GMAC concordance (published Jul 2025); section percentiles are reputable secondary (Target Test Prep), ±1-2 pts.

sectionRange.note: Each of QR / VR / DI scored on this scale; equally weighted into the total

meanTotal.note: GMAC-cited global mean total is commonly reported at ~546 (early Focus-Edition data, ~Jan 2024). Some 2024-2025 secondary sources reverse-engineer the global mean at ~554-556 over the Jul 2020-Jun 2025 population (>530k-596k test-takers). GMAC has not prominently published a single headline mean for the Focus Edition, so treat as ~546-556 (uncertain). Section means (~Quant 78, Verbal 79, DI 74-75) are more consistently cited. US test-takers score slightly below the global mean; India/China slightly above. No official country-level Focus means published.

sectionPercentiles.note: Section scaled scores run 60-90 in 1-point steps. The SAME scaled score maps to DIFFERENT percentiles per section — Quant percentiles run markedly LOWER than Verbal/DI for a given score (Quant distribution is compressed high). Points below are from Target Test Prep's GMAT Focus section charts (reflecting GMAC section data, 2024-2025). Treat as reputable secondary, ~+/-1-2 pts.
sectionPercentiles.keyTakeaway: e.g. an 84 = 85th %ile in Quant but 89th in Verbal and 97th in Data Insights; an 82 = 75th (Q) / 74th (V) / 93rd (DI). Adcoms see all three section percentiles individually.

targetScores.note: Class of 2026 average Focus score 669; admitted range ~555-765; competitive threshold ~655, strong ~685+. Source: ISB class profile via CrackVerbal/MIM-Essay.
Class of 2027 average on Focus scale ~685 (≈ old-GMAT 730, 95.8th %ile). Source: Magoosh 2026 top-programs roundup / HBS profile.
Class of 2027 ~689 (median/avg on Focus scale; Stanford historically highest, old-GMAT ~738). Source: Magoosh.
Class of 2027 ~676 on Focus scale (≈ old-GMAT ~728). Source: Magoosh.
Class of 2027 median ~687 on Focus scale (one of the highest reported). Source: Magoosh.
REVERSE-ENGINEERED/CONVERTED: INSEAD reports classic ~710 average; concordance maps ~710 to Focus ~655 (90.5th %ile). INSEAD may still publish classic. Source: TTP/MIM-Essay + GMAC concordance.

REVERSE-ENGINEERED/CONVERTED: LBS reports classic ~700-708 average; concordance maps to Focus ~645-655. LBS threshold sits slightly above ISB. Source: admissions-consultant data (LilacBuds/Menlo) + GMAC concordance.
Old-GMAT 700 concords to Focus 645 (86.7th %ile) — the widely-cited 'new 700' for top-25 competitiveness. Source: GMAC concordance.
Old-GMAT 730 concords to Focus 685 (95.8th %ile) — typical M7 average. M7 Class-of-2027 averages cluster ~670-690 on Focus. Source: GMAC concordance + Magoosh.

scoring.selectionNote: Schools read the total score, the three section scores, AND percentiles. Data Insights now carries a third of the weight, so a lopsided QR-only profile is weaker than it looks.
scoring.concordanceNote: VERIFIED from the official GMAC 'GMAT Score Concordance Table — Total Score Sorted by Percentile Ranking' PDF (©2025 GMAC, Published July 2025, data period Jul 1 2020–Jun 30 2025). Total scores run 205-805 in 10-point steps. Values rounded to nearest tenth. Concordance to old GMAT (200-800): 805/795/785/775≈800, 685≈730, 655≈700-710, 645≈700, 605≈650, 555≈600, 545≈580-590.
scoring.dataFreshness: Official GMAC concordance/percentiles published July 2025; data period July 1, 2020 - June 30, 2025. Percentiles refresh annually in Q3.

feeReference.indiaINR: Approx INR 24,780 test-center / INR 27,036 online (USD-pegged conversion; sources vary ~INR 23,000-25,600 test-center depending on FX and card charges)
feeReference.note: GMAC prices are set per country in local currency; USD figures shown are the US/most-markets standard. Standard split is USD 275 at a test center vs USD 300 online (online carries a ~USD 25 premium). Registration includes up to 5 free score reports; each extra score report is USD 35. Reschedule fees USD 50/100/150 and cancellation fees USD 165/190/220 apply by proximity to the appointment. Regional variation exists — a few markets differ from the US price.

strategy.note: The Focus Edition rewards accuracy and steady pacing over raw speed: it is adaptive, has no negative marking, and lets you edit up to 3 answers per section. Because Data Insights is now a full third of the score, treat it as a first-class section, not an afterthought.

phases.name: Foundation
phases.focus: Content review: Quant (arithmetic/algebra/word problems/stats — NO geometry), Verbal (RC + CR logic), and learn the five Data Insights formats. Take a diagnostic official mock early.

phases.name: Practice
phases.focus: Official Guide + question-bank drilling by type; build a DS answer-grid habit; log every error by type and difficulty; timed section practice.

phases.name: Mocks + Review
phases.focus: Full-length official-style mocks under real conditions; deep error-log review; refine section order and per-question pacing (~2 min/Q); target weak type until stable.

pacing.rule: Never leave a question blank — a wrong answer costs nothing but an unanswered one is scored wrong and the adaptive engine punishes trailing blanks. Guess and move on.

mocks.cadence: 1 diagnostic up front, then 1 every 1-2 weeks, 2-3 in the final fortnight
mocks.rule: Use the official GMAC practice exams for the truest score read; review each mock at least as long as you took to sit it

sectionTips.quant: Content is narrower than CAT/GRE (no geometry, no DS) but the answer choices are engineered around your most likely mistake — slow down, translate the words precisely, and check units and 'must be true'. Backsolving from the options and smart number-picking are high-value.
sectionTips.verbal: Only RC and CR. For CR, name the question type first (assumption/strengthen/weaken/etc.), find the conclusion and the gap, then attack. For RC, map structure over memorizing detail. No grammar/Sentence Correction to study anymore.
sectionTips.di: The score differentiator, and a third of your total. Master the Data Sufficiency answer grid cold, practice reading multi-tab and table/graph data fast, and drill Two-Part and Graphics formats — they feel unfamiliar but are learnable and repay practice heavily.
sectionTips.editFeature: Bookmark anything you're unsure of; if time remains at a section's end, spend it changing at most 3 flagged answers. Don't burn the whole buffer hunting for edits — bank a small cushion, review your 2-3 shakiest bookmarks, done.
sectionTips.sectionOrder: You choose the order of the three sections. Pick a consistent order in practice (many start with their strongest to bank confidence, or with DI while fresh) and keep it for the real test.

commonMistakes: Neglecting Data Insights — it is now a full third of the score, not a bonus section
Studying retired content: Sentence Correction, geometry, or the AWA essay no longer exist on the exam
Leaving questions unanswered at the end of a section (scored wrong + adaptive penalty) instead of guessing
Chasing speed over accuracy on an adaptive test where early accuracy sets your difficulty band
Misusing the edit feature — wasting time hunting edits instead of banking a cushion and fixing only the 2-3 shakiest answers
Treating a Focus score like an old-GMAT score — 645 Focus is not '645 old'; always map via GMAC's concordance
Under-practicing the Data Sufficiency answer grid, then misapplying it (especially the C-trap) under time pressure

mocks.name: GMAC Official Practice Exams 1 & 2 (Free) — Official Starter Kit
mocks.detail: Two full-length computer-adaptive exams plus ~70 sample questions, free with no registration or payment. Runs the same adaptive algorithm and official 205-805 scale as test day, so scores are the most realistic available. This is the single most trustworthy free mock — take one early as a baseline and save one for late in prep.

mocks.name: GMAC Official Practice Exams 3-6 (paid)
mocks.detail: Four additional official full-length exams (6 official exams exist in total) sold on mba.com; the best-value paid mocks because they use official questions and scoring.

mocks.name: GMAT Club Tests
mocks.detail: One free full-length adaptive GMAT Focus mock; paid packages add more CATs and a 1,300+ official-style question bank with detailed community explanations. IRT-based engine that adapts from the first question.

mocks.name: Manhattan Prep (powered by Kaplan) free practice test
mocks.detail: A free full-length GMAT practice test — email sign-up, no credit card. Note: Manhattan/Kaplan's current free offering routes to the official GMAT Focus practice tests rather than a proprietary CAT, so it overlaps with GMAC's free exams.

mocks.name: Target Test Prep (TTP) free trial + free diagnostic
mocks.detail: 5-day free trial of the full self-paced course (4,000+ practice questions, Quant/Verbal/Data Insights) with no auto-billing, plus a standalone free diagnostic test. Good for question volume and topic-level analytics.

mocks.name: Experts' Global GMAT practice tests
mocks.detail: 15 full-length GMAT Focus mocks with the first one free. Fixed 64-question format (approx. 7 easy / 31 medium / 26 hard) rather than adaptive — useful for stamina and exposure but calibrate the score against an official mock.

mocks.name: e-GMAT free Focus mock
mocks.detail: A free GMAT Focus mock to establish a baseline score; part of e-GMAT's Scholaranium/free resources ecosystem.

reading: GMAT Official Guide (latest edition — 2025-2026; the 2024-2025 is also current and cheaper): the flagship book + online question bank, and the ONLY source of real retired official GMAT questions. Non-negotiable core resource.

GMAT Official Guide Bundle (OG + Quantitative Review + Verbal Review + Data Insights Review + online question bank): ~1,600+ official questions; the three section-specific review books add 200+ Quant, 250+ Verbal and 250+ Data Insights questions NOT in the main OG.
GMAT Official Guide Data Insights Review: a dedicated book of official DI questions — important because Data Insights is now equally weighted and under-covered by older prep material.

Manhattan Prep GMAT strategy guides: strong for building underlying concepts and test strategy — buy the current GMAT Focus editions (older sets waste chapters on removed Sentence Correction/geometry).

Target Test Prep (online course, not a book): the most recommended paid self-study platform — 52 chapters, 1,500+ lessons, 4,000+ questions across Quant, Verbal and Data Insights; best for structured Quant/DI improvement.
e-GMAT (online): popular with non-native English speakers for its scaffolded Verbal and Data Insights methods.
communities: GMAT Club (gmatclub.com): the largest GMAT forum — free tests, official-question explanations from experts, error log, timer, decision tracker and B-school threads. Central hub for GMAT Focus discussion.
r/GMAT (Reddit): active peer community for debriefs, score reports, resource recommendations and moral support; r/GMATpreparation is a related sub for strategies and free resources.
Beat The GMAT: long-running forum plus independent course/tutor reviews and scholarship listings.
Manhattan Prep GMAT Forum: instructor-answered Q&A on Official Guide problems and strategy.
Discord / Telegram study groups: various community-run servers (linked from GMAT Club and r/GMAT) for accountability and real-time study-buddy help.
